blocks:
Ayahs

حمٓ (١)
Ha, Meem, (1)
عٓسٓقٓ (٢)
‘Ayn, Seen, Qaf. (2)
كَذَٰلِكَ يُوحِىٓ إِلَيْكَ وَإِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِكَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٣)
Allah, the All-mighty and the All-wise, thus reveals to you and to those who were before you: (3)
لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَلِىُّ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (٤)
whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth belongs to Him, and He is the All-exalted, the All-supreme. (4)
تَكَادُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتُ يَتَفَطَّرْنَ مِن فَوْقِهِنَّ ۚ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْغَفُورُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٥)
The heavens are about to be rent apart from above them, while the angels celebrate the praise of their Lord and plead for forgiveness for those [faithful] who are on the earth. Indeed, Allah is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful! (5)
وَٱلَّذِينَ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ ٱللَّهُ حَفِيظٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَمَآ أَنتَ عَلَيْهِم بِوَكِيلٍ (٦)
As for those who have taken awliya besides Him, Allah is watchful over them, and you are not their keeper. (6)

(Commentary)

The Surah discusses revelation (الوحي), which is a mode of Allah’s speech to His prophets and messengers, as indicated by the opening verse: كَذلِكَ يُوحِي إِلَيْكَ وَإِلَى الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ اللهُ "Thus does Allah... reveal to you and to those before you" (42:3), and the concluding verse: وَما كانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَنْ يُكَلِّمَهُ اللهُ إِلَّا وَحْياً "It is not [fitting] for any human that Allah should speak to him except by revelation..." (42:51). The discourse returns to this theme repeatedly, as in His saying: وَكَذلِكَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا "Thus have We revealed to you an Arabic Quran" (42:7), and His saying:

شَرَعَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الدِّينِ ما وَصَّى بِهِ نُوحاً "He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah" (42:13), and His saying: اللهُ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ وَالْمِيزانَ "It is Allah who has sent down the Book with the truth and the Balance" (42:17), as well as the recurring mentions of provision (الرزق) in the Surah, as will be discussed.

Thus, revelation (الوحي) is the primary subject around which the discourse of the Surah revolves. The references to the signs of monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts —, the descriptions of the believers and the disbelievers, and what awaits both groups in their return (المعاد) to Allah, the Glorified, are secondary objectives and tangential discussions.

The Surah is Meccan (مكية), with the exception of His saying: وَالَّذِينَ اسْتَجابُوا لِرَبِّهِمْ "Those who answer their Lord" (42:38) until the end of three verses, and His saying: قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى "Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it except love of [my] relatives’" (42:23) until the end of four verses. Discussion regarding these will follow, Allah the Exalted willing.

His saying, the Exalted: حم عسق (Ha Meem, ‘Ayn Seen Qaf) consists of the disjointed letters (الحروف المقطعة) found at the beginning of several Quranic Surahs. This is a unique feature of the Noble Quran, not found in any other heavenly books.

Both early and later exegetes have differed regarding their interpretation. Al-Tabarsi, in Majma‘ al-Bayan, cited eleven opinions concerning their meaning:

First: They are among the ambiguous verses (المتشابهات) — verses whose precise meaning is not immediately clear and may be open to multiple interpretations — whose knowledge Allah has reserved for Himself; none knows their interpretation (تأويل) except Him.

Second: Each is a name for the Surah at whose beginning it occurs.

Third: They are names for the Quran, meaning the entire collection.

Fourth: They are intended to indicate the names of Allah, the Exalted. Thus, الم means "I am Allah, the All-Knowing" (أنا الله أعلم); المر means "I am Allah, the All-Knowing, the All-Seeing" (أنا الله أعلم وأرى); المص means "I am Allah, the All-Knowing, the Decider" (أنا الله أعلم وأفصل); and كهيعص means the Kaf is from the Sufficient (الكافي), the Ha from the Guide (الهادي), the Ya from the Wise (الحكيم), the ‘Ayn from the All-Knowing (العليم), and the Sad from the Truthful (الصادق). This is narrated from Ibn Abbas. The letters taken from the names vary in their derivation: some are from the beginning of the name, like the Kaf from al-Kafi; some from the middle, like the Ya from al-Hakim; and some from the end, like the Meem from A‘lam.

Fifth: They are disjointed names of Allah, the Exalted, which, if people knew how to compose them, they would know the Greatest Name (اسم الله الأعظم) of Allah. For instance, Alif-Lam-Ra, Ha-Meem, and Nun form al-Rahman (الرحمن). The same applies to the rest, except that we are unable to compose them. This is narrated from Sa‘id ibn Jubayr.

Sixth: They are oaths by which Allah has sworn. It is as if He swore by these letters that the Quran is His speech. They are noble because they are the foundations of His revealed books, His Beautiful Names (أسماؤه الحسنى), His Sublime Attributes (صفاته العليا), and the origins of the languages of various nations.

Seventh: They are indications of Allah’s blessings (آلاء), His trials (بلاء), the duration of nations, and their lifespans and terms.

Eighth: They are intended to indicate the duration of this Ummah based on the numerical value of the letters (حساب الجمل).

Ninth: They refer to the letters of the alphabet, and by mentioning some, He suffices from mentioning the rest, just as one says "ABC" to mean all the letters.

Tenth: They were intended to silence the disbelievers. This is because the polytheists (المشركين) had exhorted one another not to listen to the Quran and to make noise during its recitation, as the Quran recounts: لا تَسْمَعُوا لِهذَا الْقُرْآآنِ وَالْغَوْا فِيهِ "Do not listen to this Quran, and make a noise during its recitation" (41:26). They would whistle, clap, or speak loudly to confuse the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله during his recitation. Thus, Allah revealed these letters so that when they heard them, they would find them strange, listen to them, reflect upon them, and be distracted from their noise, allowing the Quran to reach their ears.

Eleventh: They are like the enumeration of the letters of the alphabet, intended to show that this Quran, which you are unable to challenge, is composed of the same letters you use in your speeches and conversations. Since you cannot produce its like, know that it is from Allah, the Exalted. The letters were repeated in various places to reinforce the argument. This is narrated from Qutrub and chosen by Abu Muslim al-Isfahani, and many later scholars lean toward it.

These are eleven opinions. Among what has been narrated from them, another opinion could be formed, such as what was narrated from Ibn Abbas regarding الم that the Alif refers to Allah, the Lam to Gabriel, and the Meem to Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. Some also suggested that the disjointed letters at the beginning of the Surahs indicate the primary purpose explained therein; for example, Nun refers to the promised victory (النصر) for the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and Qaf refers to the Quran or the Divine Subjugation (القهر الإلهي) mentioned in the Surah. Others suggested these letters are for awakening (الإيقاظ) the listener.

The truth is that none of these opinions provide complete satisfaction to the soul:

As for the first opinion, it was previously discussed in the inquiry regarding the clear (المحكم) and ambiguous (المتشابه) verses at the beginning of the third volume of this book. It was explained there that clarity (الإحكام) and ambiguity (التشابه) are attributes of verses that have a linguistic indication (دلالة لفظية) of their meanings, and that interpretation (التأويل) is not of the nature of linguistic meanings. Rather, interpretations are objective realities (حقائق واقعية) that emanate from the contents of the Quranic statements, whether clear or ambiguous. Accordingly, these disjointed letters are not "ambiguous verses," nor are the meanings intended by them their "interpretations."

As for the other ten opinions, they are merely conceptualizations that do not exceed the level of possibility (الاحتمال), and there is no evidence to prove any of them.

Yes, in some narrations attributed to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه‌السلام, there is some support for the fourth, seventh, eighth, and tenth opinions. These will be cited and their implications discussed in the upcoming "Tradition Inquiry," Allah the Exalted willing.

What should not be overlooked is that these letters are repeated in various Surahs, totaling twenty-nine Surahs. Some begin with a single letter, namely Sad, Qaf, and Nun. Some begin with two letters, namely Ta-Ha, Ta-Seen, Ya-Seen, and the Ha-Meem Surahs. Some begin with three letters, as in the Surahs beginning with Alif-Lam-Meem, Alif-Lam-Ra, and Ta-Seen-Meem. Some begin with four letters, as in Alif-Lam-Meem-Sad and Alif-Lam-Meem-Ra. Some begin with five letters, as in Kaf-Ha-Ya-‘Ayn-Sad and Ha-Meem ‘Ayn-Seen-Qaf.

These letters also differ in that some occur only in one place, like Nun, while others occur at the beginning of several Surahs, like Alif-Lam-Meem, Alif-Lam-Ra, Ta-Seen, and Ha-Meem.

Furthermore, if you reflect even slightly upon these Surahs that share the same opening letters—such as the Meems, the Ras, the Ta-Seens, and the Ha-Meems—you will find in the Surahs sharing these letters a similarity in themes and a harmony in context (السياقات) that does not exist between them and other Surahs.

This is reinforced by the similarity of phrasing at the beginning of most of them. For instance, the Ha-Meem Surahs begin with: تَنْزِيلُ الْكِتابِ مِنَ اللهِ "The sent-down [revelation] of the Book is from Allah" (45:2) or its equivalent. The Ra Surahs begin with: تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ "These are the signs of the Book" (10:1) or its equivalent. A similar pattern occurs in the Ta-Seen Surahs, and the Meem Surahs begin by negating doubt (الريب) about the Book or its equivalent.

From this, one might intuit that there is a specific connection between these disjointed letters and the contents of the Surahs they initiate. This is supported by finding that Surah al-A‘raf, which begins with Alif-Lam-Meem-Sad, seems to combine the themes of the Meem Surahs and Surah Sad. Similarly, Surah al-Ra‘d, which begins with Alif-Lam-Meem-Ra, seems to combine the themes of the Meem Surahs and the Ra Surahs.

It can be inferred from this that these letters are symbols (رموز) between Allah, the Glorified, and His Messenger صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, hidden from us. Our ordinary intellects have no way to reach them, except to the extent that we sense a specific connection between them and the contents deposited in the Surahs.

Perhaps if a reflective person were to ponder the commonalities of these letters and compare the contents of the Surahs in which they occur, the matter would become clearer.

Perhaps this is the meaning of what the Sunnis narrated from Ali عليه‌السلام — as mentioned in al-Majma‘ — that "Every book has a choice part (صفوة), and the choice part of this Book is the letters of the alphabet..."

His saying, the Exalted: كَذلِكَ يُوحِي إِلَيْكَ وَإِلَى الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ اللهُ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ "Thus does Allah, the All-mighty, the All-wise, reveal to you and to those before you" (42:3) until His saying: الْعَلِيُّ الْعَظِيمُ "the All-exalted, the All-supreme" (42:4). Since the purpose of the Surah is to explain revelation (الوحي) by defining its reality and indicating its goal and effects, the reference in His saying كَذلِكَ "Thus" points to the specific instance of revelation—the conveying of this Surah to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. This serves as a definition of revelation in general by likening it to a specific, observed instance present to the addressee, much like saying in the definition of a human, for example, "he is like Zayd."

Accordingly, His saying إِلَيْكَ وَإِلَى الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِكَ "to you and to those before you" means "to all of you." It is expressed this way to indicate that revelation is an established Divine practice (سنة إلهية) and not an innovation. The meaning is: the revelation We reveal to you, the assembly of prophets—prophet after prophet as an ongoing practice—is like this which you experience and witness in the reception of this Surah.

The majority of exegetes have taken كَذلِكَ "Thus" as a reference to revelation not in itself, but in terms of the message it contains. Thus, it is actually a reference to the knowledge (المعارف) contained and included in the Surah. They concluded from this that the content of the Surah is among what Allah, the Exalted, revealed to all prophets, making it part of the shared revelation. However, as you have seen, this does not align with the primary purpose of the Surah and is resisted by the context of its verses.

His saying: الْعَلِيُّ الْعَظِيمُ لَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ وَهُوَ الْعَلِيُّ الْعَظِيمُ mentions five of His Beautiful Names. His saying لَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ "To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth" (42:4) carries the meaning of Ownership (المالك). This serves as a justification (تعليل) for the origin of revelation and its being an ongoing Divine practice. That which revelation provides is a Divine law (شرع إلهي) containing guidance for people toward the happiness of their lives in this world and the Hereafter. There is nothing to prevent Him, the Exalted, from this, for He is the All-mighty (العزيز) — the one who is never overcome in what He wills. Nor does He, the Exalted, neglect the guidance of His servants, for He is the All-wise (الحكيم) — the one whose acts are perfect and precise. Part of the perfection of an act is that it be led to its ultimate goal.

It is His right, the Exalted, to dispose of them and their affairs as He wills, for He is their Owner (مالكهم). He has the right to make them worship Him and to enslave them through command and prohibition because He is the All-exalted (العلي) and the All-supreme (العظيم). Thus, each of the five names has its share in the justification, and their collective result is that He is their Guardian (ولي) from every aspect, and there is no guardian besides Him.

His saying, the Exalted: تَكادُ السَّماواتُ يَتَفَطَّرْنَ مِنْ فَوْقِهِنَ "The heavens well-nigh crack open from above..." (42:5). التفطر (splitting) is derived from al-fatr (الفطر), meaning a crack or split.

What the context suggests—as the discourse is arranged to explain the reality of revelation, its goal, and its effects—is that the cracking of the heavens from above refers to their splitting due to the revelation descending from Allah, the All-exalted, the All-supreme, passing through them heaven by heaven until it descends to the earth. For the origin of revelation is Allah, the Glorified, and the heavens are paths to the earth. He, the Exalted, said: وَلَقَدْ خَلَقْنا فَوْقَكُمْ سَبْعَ طَرائِقَ وَما كُنَّا عَنِ الْخَلْقِ غافِلِينَ "Certainly We have created above you seven paths, and We have not been oblivious of the creation" (23:17).

The reason for qualifying يَتَفَطَّرْنَ "crack open" with His saying مِنْ فَوْقِهِنَ "from above them" is clear, for revelation descends upon them from above them, from the One who possesses absolute highness and absolute greatness. If they were to crack, it would be from above them.

This also serves to magnify the matter of revelation and exalt it, for it is the speech of the All-exalted, the All-supreme. Because it is the speech of the One with absolute greatness, the heavens well-nigh crack open at its descent; and because it is speech descending from the One with absolute highness, they would crack from above them, if they were to crack.

Thus, the verse magnifies the matter of Allah’s speech in terms of its descent and passage through the heavens. It is similar to His saying: حَتَّى إِذا فُزِّعَ عَنْ قُلُوبِهِمْ قالُوا ما ذا قالَ رَبُّكُمْ قالُوا الْحَقَّ وَهُوَ الْعَلِيُّ الْكَبِيرُ "When fear is lifted from their hearts, they say, ‘What did your Lord say?’ They say, ‘The truth, and He is the All-exalted, the All-great’" (34:23) in magnifying it from the perspective of the angels of the heavens receiving it. It is also similar to His saying: لَوْ أَنْزَلْنا هذَا الْقُرْآآنَ عَلى جَبَلٍ لَرَأَيْتَهُ خاشِعاً مُتَصَدِّعاً مِنْ خَشْيَةِ اللهِ "Had We sent down this Quran upon a mountain, you would have surely seen it humbled [and] go to pieces with the fear of Allah" (59:21) in magnifying it on the assumption of its descent upon a mountain. And it is similar to His saying: إِنَّا سَنُلْقِي عَلَيْكَ قَوْلاً ثَقِيلاً "Soon We shall cast on you a weighty word" (73:5) regarding its weightiness and the difficulty of bearing it. This is what the context provides.

The exegetes have interpreted the verse according to one of two other meanings:

First: That the intended meaning is their cracking from the greatness and majesty of Allah, may His majesty be glorified, as supported by His being described previously as the All-exalted, the All-supreme.

Second: That the intended meaning is their cracking from the polytheism (شرك) of the polytheists among the people of the earth and their saying: اتَّخَذَ الرَّحْمنُ وَلَداً "The All-beneficent has taken a son" (19:88). For He, the Exalted, said regarding this: تَكادُ السَّماواتُ يَتَفَطَّرْنَ مِنْهُ "The heavens are about to be rent apart at it" (19:90). This led to strained efforts (التكلف) in justifying the qualification of the cracking with His saying مِنْ فَوْقِهِنَ "from above them," especially according to the second meaning, as well as in justifying the connection of His saying وَالْمَلائِكَةُ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ "and the angels celebrate the praise of their Lord and plead for forgiveness for those who are on the earth" (42:5) with what precedes it, as is not hidden from those who consult their books.

His saying: وَالْمَلائِكَةُ يُسَبِّحُونَ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّهِمْ وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ means they declare Him transcendent (ينزهونه) from what is not befitting His sanctity and they praise Him for His beautiful acts. Among what is not befitting His sanctity is that He should neglect the affair of His servants and not guide them through a religion He prescribes for them via revelation, which is a beautiful act from Him. They ask Him, the Exalted, to forgive the people of the earth. The attainment of forgiveness (المغفرة) only occurs through the attainment of its cause, which is following the path of servitude (العبودية) by being guided by the guidance of Allah, the Glorified. Thus, their asking for forgiveness for them returns to asking that He prescribe for them a religion through which He forgives those among them who practice it. So the meaning is: the angels ask Allah, the Glorified, to prescribe for those on earth, through revelation, a religion they may follow, so that He may forgive them thereby.

Evidence for this meaning is the occurrence of the sentence in the context of describing the attribute of revelation, as well as the connection of seeking forgiveness to "those on the earth." For there is no meaning in seeking forgiveness for all the people of the earth, even for those who said: "Allah has taken a son." Allah, the Exalted, has recounted regarding them: وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا "and plead for forgiveness for those who have faith" (40:7). Thus, it is necessary to interpret the request for forgiveness as a request for its cause, which is the prescription of religion for the people of the earth so that those who practice it may be forgiven.

His saying: أَلا إِنَّ اللهَ هُوَ الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ "Behold! Allah is indeed the All-forgiving, the All-merciful" (42:5) means that because Allah, the Glorified, is characterized by the attributes of forgiveness and mercy and is named by the names the All-forgiving (الغفور) and the All-merciful (الرحيم), it is befitting His sanctity that He act toward the people of the earth in a way that they may attain forgiveness and mercy from Him. This is by prescribing for them a religion through which they are guided to their happiness via revelation and speech.

It is said: In His saying أَلا إِنَّ اللهَ etc., there is an indication of the acceptance of the angels' seeking of forgiveness, and that He, the Glorified, grants them more than what they asked for in terms of forgiveness and mercy.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالَّذِينَ اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِهِ أَوْلِياءَ اللهُ حَفِيظٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَما أَنْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ بِوَكِيلٍ "As for those who take guardians besides Him, Allah is watchful over them, and you are not their guardian" (42:6). Since it was understood from the previous verses that Allah, the Exalted, is the Guardian (الولي) of His servants and there is no guardian besides Him, and that He takes charge of the affairs of those on earth by prescribing for them a religion He approves through revelation to His prophets—as necessitated by His Beautiful Names and Sublime Attributes—and since this necessitates that His servants not take guardians besides Him, He pointed out in this verse the state of those who take guardians besides Him by taking partners for Him in Lordship (الربوبية) and Divinity (الألوهية). He mentioned that He is not oblivious to what they do, and that their deeds are preserved (محفوظة) against them and they will be held accountable for them. The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is only responsible for the delivery of the message (البلاغ), without being a guardian (وكيل) over them responsible for their deeds.

So His saying: اللهُ حَفِيظٌ عَلَيْهِمْ means He preserves against them their polytheism and the evil deeds that branch from it.

His saying: وَما أَنْتَ عَلَيْهِمْ بِوَكِيلٍ means their deeds are not delegated to you such that you must rectify them by guiding them to the truth. The speech is not without a kind of consolation (تسلية) for the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Ishaq, al-Bukhari in his Tarikh, and Ibn Jarir narrated through a weak chain from Ibn Abbas from Jabir ibn Abdillah ibn Rabab, who said: Abu Yasir ibn Akhtab passed by the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله while he was reciting the opening of Surah al-Baqarah: الم ذلِكَ الْكِتابُ "Alif, Lam, Meem. This is the Book..." (2:1-2). Then his brother Huyayy ibn Akhtab came with a group of Jews and said: "Do you know? By Allah, I heard Muhammad reciting from what was revealed to him: الم ذلِكَ الْكِتابُ." They said: "You heard him?" He said: "Yes."

So those people walked to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and said: "O Muhammad, did you not mention that you recite from what was revealed to you: الم ذلِكَ الْكِتابُ?" He said: "Yes." They said: "Did Gabriel bring this to you from Allah?" He said: "Yes." They said: "Allah sent prophets before you, and we do not know of Him clarifying for any prophet the duration of his kingdom and the term of his Ummah except you."

Then Huyayy ibn Akhtab said, turning to those with him: "The Alif is one, the Lam is thirty, and the Meem is forty; this is seventy-one years. Would you enter the religion of a prophet whose kingdom's duration and his Ummah's term is only seventy-one years?"

Then he turned to the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and said: "O Muhammad, is there anything else with this?" He said: "Yes." He asked: "What is it?" He said: (المص). He said: "This is heavier and longer. The Alif is one, the Lam is thirty, the Meem is forty, and the Sad is ninety; this is one hundred and sixty-one years. Is there anything else with this, O Muhammad?" He said: "Yes." He asked: "What is it?" He said: (الر). He said: "This is heavier and longer. The Alif is one, the Lam is thirty, and the Ra is two hundred; this is two hundred and thirty-one years. Is there anything else with this?" He said: "Yes." He asked: "What is it?" He said: (المر). He said: "This is heavier and longer. The Alif is one, the Lam is thirty, the Meem is forty, and the Ra is two hundred; this is two hundred and seventy-one years."

Then he said: "Your matter has become confused for us, O Muhammad, until we do not know whether you were given a little or a lot." Then they stood up, and Abu Yasir said to his brother Huyayy and the rabbis with him: "How do you know? Perhaps all of this has been gathered for Muhammad: seventy-one, one hundred and sixty-one, two hundred and thirty-one, and two hundred and seventy-one; that makes seven hundred and thirty-four." They said: "His matter has become ambiguous (تشابه) for us." They claim that these verses were revealed concerning them: هُوَ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ عَلَيْكَ الْكِتابَ مِنْهُ آياتٌ مُحْكَماتٌ هُنَّ أُمُّ الْكِتابِ وَأُخَرُ مُتَشابِهاتٌ "It is He who has sent down to you the Book. Parts of it are definitive verses, which are the mother of the Book, and others are ambiguous" (3:7).

I say: A similar report was narrated from Ibn al-Mundhir from Ibn Jurayj. Al-Qummi also narrated its like in his Tafsir, from his father, from Ibn Ri’ab, from Muhammad ibn Qays, from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him). There is nothing in the narration to indicate the Prophet’s صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله endorsement of their claim, nor did they have any proof for what they claimed. It has already been mentioned that the ambiguous verses (الآيات المتشابهة) are not the disjointed letters at the beginning of the Surahs.

In al-Ma‘ani, through his chain from Juwayriyah from Sufyan al-Thawri, who said: I said to Ja‘far ibn Muhammad ibn Ali ibn al-Husayn ibn Ali ibn Abi Talib عليه‌السلام: "O son of the Messenger of Allah, what is the meaning of the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: (الم) and (المص) and (الر) and (المر) and (كهيعص) and (طه) and (طس) and (طسم) and (يس) and (ص) and (حم) and (حم عسق) and (ق) and (ن)?"

He (peace be upon him) said: "As for (الم) at the beginning of al-Baqarah, its meaning is 'I am Allah, the King' (أنا الله الملك). As for (الم) at the beginning of Al ‘Imran, its meaning is 'I am Allah, the Glorious' (أنا الله المجيد). (المص) means 'I am Allah, the All-powerful, the Truthful' (أنا الله المقتدر الصادق). (الر) means 'I am Allah, the All-kind' (أنا الله الرءوف). (المر) means 'I am Allah, the Giver of life, the Bringer of death, the Provider' (أنا الله المحيي المميت الرازق). (كهيعص) means 'I am the Sufficient, the Guide, the Guardian, the All-knowing, the Truthful in promise' (أنا الكافي الهادي الولي العالم الصادق الوعد). As for (طه), it is a name from the names of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and its meaning is 'O seeker of the truth, the guide to it' (يا طالب الحق الهادي إليه); We did not reveal the Quran to you that you should be miserable, but that you should be happy through it.

As for (طس), its meaning is 'I am the Seeker, the All-hearing' (أنا الطالب السميع). As for (طسم), its meaning is 'I am the Seeker, the All-hearing, the Originator, the Restorer' (أنا الطالب السميع المبدئ المعيد). As for (يس), it is a name from the names of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and its meaning is 'O you who hears the revelation and the Wise Quran, indeed you are among the messengers, on a straight path.'

As for (ص), it is a spring that gushes from beneath the Throne. It is that from which the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله performed ablution when he was taken on the Night Journey. Gabriel enters it every day once, immerses himself in it, then comes out and shakes his wings. Not a drop falls from his wings but that Allah, Blessed and Exalted, creates from it an angel who glorifies Allah, sanctifies Him, magnifies Him, and praises Him until the Day of Resurrection.

As for (حم), its meaning is 'the All-praiseworthy, the Glorious' (الحميد المجيد). As for (حم عسق), its meaning is 'the All-forbearing, the Rewarder, the All-knowing, the All-hearing, the All-powerful, the All-strong' (الحليم المثيب العالم السميع القادر القوي). As for (ق), it is the mountain surrounding the earth, and the greenness of the sky is from it; by it, Allah holds the earth lest it sway with its inhabitants. As for (ن), it is a river in Paradise. Allah, Mighty and Majestic, said: 'Freeze,' and it froze and became ink (مداد). Then He, Mighty and Majestic, said to the Pen: 'Write.' So the Pen inscribed on the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ) what was and what will be until the Day of Resurrection. The ink is ink of light, the Pen is a pen of light, and the Tablet is a tablet of light."

Sufyan said: I said to him: "O son of the Messenger of Allah, clarify for me the matter of the Tablet, the Pen, and the ink with further clarification, and teach me from what Allah has taught you." He said: "O son of Sa‘id, were you not worthy of an answer, I would not have answered you. Nun is an angel who delivers to the Pen, which is an angel; the Pen delivers to the Tablet, which is an angel; the Tablet delivers to Israfil; Israfil delivers to Michael; Michael delivers to Gabriel; and Gabriel delivers to the prophets and messengers صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله." He said: Then he said to me: "Stand up, O Sufyan, for I do not feel secure for you [from the weight of this knowledge]..."

I say: The apparent meaning of the narration's interpretation of most disjointed letters as the Beautiful Names of Allah is that they are letters taken from the names—either from their beginning, like the Meem from al-Malik, al-Majid, and al-Muqtadir, or from among their letters, like the Lam from Allah and the Ya from al-Wali. Thus, the disjointed letters would be symbolic indications (إشارات على سبيل الرمز) of the names of Allah, the Exalted. This meaning has been narrated through Sunni chains from Ibn Abbas, al-Rabi‘ ibn Anas, and others. However, it is not hidden from you that symbolism (الرمز) in speech is only resorted to for disclosing matters that the speaker does not want anyone other than the addressee to know. Thus, he symbolizes it with something that does not go beyond him and his addressee, and no one else understands it. Yet these Beautiful Names have been mentioned and clarified in many places in His speech, the Exalted, explicitly, implicitly, generally, and in detail. Given this, there would be no benefit in referring to each of them with a letter taken from it as a symbol.

Therefore, the proper view—assuming the narration is authentic—is to take these letters as indicating these meanings in a non-conventional (غير وضعية) manner. They would be symbols for them, hidden from us and unknown to us, indicating levels of these meanings that are finer, loftier, and more elevated than our understandings. This is somewhat supported by interpreting the same letter, like Meem, in different places with different meanings, as well as what has been narrated that they are among the letters of the Greatest Name of Allah.

His saying: "As for (ق), it is the mountain surrounding the earth, and the greenness of the sky is from it" etc. Al-Qummi narrated something similar in his Tafsir, and it is narrated through several Sunni chains from Ibn Abbas and others. The wording of some is: "a mountain of emerald surrounding the world on the two sides (كنفي) (1) of the sky." In some, it is a mountain surrounding the ocean that surrounds the earth, and the lowest sky hovers over it, and there are seven earths, seven seas, seven mountains, and seven heavens.

In some of what is from Ibn Abbas: "Allah created a mountain called Qaf surrounding the world, and its roots reach the Rock upon which the earth rests. When Allah wishes to cause an earthquake in a village, He commands that mountain, and it moves the root adjacent to that village, so it quakes and moves. Thus, one village moves and not another."

These narrations, in their literal sense, are more akin to the Isra’iliyyat (إسرائيليات) — narratives of Judeo-Christian origin. Were it not for his saying: "and by it, Allah holds the earth lest it sway with its inhabitants," it would be possible to interpret his saying: "As for (ق), it is the mountain surrounding the world, and the greenness of the sky is from it" as referring to the atmosphere surrounding the earth through a kind of interpretation.

As for his saying that Ta-Ha and Ya-Seen are among the names of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله in the sense he explained, it should also be understood as we previously mentioned, and this interprets the many narrations from both Sunni and Shi‘i sources stating that Ta-Ha and Ya-Seen are names of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

As for his saying regarding Nun that it is a river which Allah turned into ink with which the Pen wrote by His command upon the Tablet what was and what will be until the Day of Resurrection, and that the ink, the Pen, and the Tablet are of light, then his saying that the ink is an angel, the Pen is an angel, and the Tablet is an angel—this is an excellent witness that what occurs in His speech, the Exalted, regarding the Throne (العرش), the Chair (الكرسي), the Tablet (اللوح), the Pen (القلم), and their likes—and was interpreted as it was in the speech of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه‌السلام—is by way of representation (التمثيل). It is intended to bring true knowledge (معارف حقيقية), which is higher and loftier than the level of general understanding, closer by presenting it in the guise of the sensible.

In al-Ma‘ani, also through his chain from Abu Basir from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: "(الم) is a letter from the letters of the Greatest Name of Allah—the disjointed one in the Quran which the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the Imam compose; when he supplicates with it, he is answered." [The hadith continues].

I say: The idea that these disjointed letters are from the letters of the Greatest Name of Allah, disjointed in the Quran, is narrated through several Sunni chains from Ibn Abbas and others. It was clarified in the inquiry regarding the Beautiful Names in Surah al-A‘raf that the Greatest Name, which has its specific effect, is not of the nature of words, and that what has been narrated whose literal sense is that it is a name composed of spoken letters is diverted from its literal sense in a manner appropriate to it.

In the same source, through his chain from Muhammad ibn Ziyad and Muhammad ibn Sayyar from al-Askari عليه‌السلام, that he said: "The Quraysh and the Jews belied the Quran and said: 'It is clear magic that he has composed.' So Allah said: الم ذلِكَ الْكِتابُ meaning: 'O Muhammad, this Book that We have sent down to you is [composed of] the disjointed letters, among which are Alif, Lam, Meem. It is in your language and your alphabet; so produce its like if you are truthful, and seek help for that from the rest of your witnesses.'" [The hadith continues].

I say: The hadith is from the Tafsir of al-Askari, which is weak.

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja‘far (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: يَتَفَطَّرْنَ مِنْ فَوْقِهِنَ "crack open from above them," he said: "they split apart."

From Jawami‘ al-Jami‘ regarding the saying of the Exalted: وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَ لِمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ "and plead for forgiveness for those who are on the earth," Imam al-Sadiq (peace be upon him) said: "For the believers among those on the earth."

I say: Something similar was narrated in al-Majma‘ from him (peace be upon him), and al-Qummi narrated it as an unattributed (مضمراً) report.

(1) Al-Kanaf (with two fathas) means the side, and the two kanafs of the sky are its two sides.

Ayahs

وَكَذَٰلِكَ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ قُرْءَانًا عَرَبِيًّا لِّتُنذِرَ أُمَّ ٱلْقُرَىٰ وَمَنْ حَوْلَهَا وَتُنذِرَ يَوْمَ ٱلْجَمْعِ لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ ۚ فَرِيقٌ فِى ٱلْجَنَّةِ وَفَرِيقٌ فِى ٱلسَّعِيرِ (٧)
Thus have We revealed to you an Arabic Quran that you may warn [the people of] the Mother of the Towns and those around it, and warn [them] of the Day of Gathering, in which there is no doubt, [whereupon] a part [of mankind] will be in paradise and a part will be in the Blaze. (7)
وَلَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ لَجَعَلَهُمْ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً وَلَـٰكِن يُدْخِلُ مَن يَشَآءُ فِى رَحْمَتِهِۦ ۚ وَٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ مَا لَهُم مِّن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ (٨)
Had Allah wished, He would have surely made them one community; but He admits whomever He wishes into His mercy, and the wrongdoers do not have any guardian or helper. (8)
أَمِ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ أَوْلِيَآءَ ۖ فَٱللَّهُ هُوَ ٱلْوَلِىُّ وَهُوَ يُحْىِ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٩)
Have they taken awliya besides Him? [Say,] ‘It is Allah, who is the [true] Wali, and He revives the dead, and He has power over all things. (9)
وَمَا ٱخْتَلَفْتُمْ فِيهِ مِن شَىْءٍ فَحُكْمُهُۥٓ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ ۚ ذَٰلِكُمُ ٱللَّهُ رَبِّى عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ وَإِلَيْهِ أُنِيبُ (١٠)
Whatever thing you may differ about, its judgement is with Allah. That is Allah, my Lord. In Him, I have put my trust, and to Him do I turn penitently. (10)
فَاطِرُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ جَعَلَ لَكُم مِّنْ أَنفُسِكُمْ أَزْوَٰجًا وَمِنَ ٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ أَزْوَٰجًا ۖ يَذْرَؤُكُمْ فِيهِ ۚ لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِۦ شَىْءٌ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْبَصِيرُ (١١)
The originator of the heavens and the earth, He made for you mates from your own selves, and mates of the cattle, by which means He multiplies you. Nothing is like Him, and He is the All-hearing, the All-seeing. (11)
لَهُۥ مَقَالِيدُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ يَبْسُطُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيَقْدِرُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَلِيمٌ (١٢)
To Him belong the keys of the heavens and the earth: He expands the provision for whomever He wishes, and tightens it [for whomever He wishes]. Indeed He has knowledge of all things.’ (12)

(Commentary)

This is a second section of verses in which revelation (الوحي) is defined by its intended purpose and ultimate goal, just as it was defined in the previous section by reference to its own essence.

In this section, it is clarified that the purpose of revelation is to warn mankind, specifically regarding the Day of Gathering (يوم الجمع), wherein people will be divided into two groups: a group in Paradise and a group in the Blaze. For were it not for the warning of the Day of Gathering—in which there is reckoning (الحساب) and recompense (الجزاء)—no religious call would succeed, nor would any conveyance of the message be effective.

Then, it explains that their separation into two groups is what Allah, the Glorified, has willed. Consequently, He followed this by legislating the religion (الدين) and warning mankind of the Day of Gathering through revelation, because He is their Guardian (الولي) — the one who takes charge of their affairs and manages their interests — who brings them to life after their death and judges between them in that which they differed.

The discourse then proceeds to the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His creative and governing authority — establishing that He, the Exalted, is the Lord (الرب) and there is no lord besides Him, due to His exclusive possession of the attributes of Lordship without any partner sharing in any part of them.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكَذلِكَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لِتُنْذِرَ أُمَّ الْقُرى وَمَنْ حَوْلَها — "Thus have We revealed to you an Arabic Quran that you may warn the Mother of Cities and those around it" (42:7). The demonstrative "thus" (كذلك) refers to the revelation understood from the preceding context. The "Mother of Cities" (أم القرى) is the honored city of Mecca, and the intent of warning the Mother of Cities is warning its inhabitants. "Those around it" (من حولها) refers to the rest of the people of the Arabian Peninsula who were outside of Mecca, as supported by the description of the Quran as being "Arabic" (عربياً).

This is because the prophetic call (الدعوة النبوية) had stages in its expansion. The public call began with the invitation of the nearest kin, as He said: وَأَنْذِرْ عَشِيرَتَكَ الْأَقْرَبِينَ — "Warn your nearest relations" (26:214). Then it expanded to encompass the Arabs in general, as He said: قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لِقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ — "an Arabic Quran for a people who have knowledge" (41:3). Finally, it extended to all of mankind, as He said: وَأُوحِيَ إِلَيَّ هذَا الْقُرْآنُ لِأُنْذِرَكُمْ بِهِ وَمَنْ بَلَغَ — "this Quran has been revealed to me that I may warn you thereby and whomever it may reach" (6:19).

Among the proofs for what we have mentioned regarding the command for gradual expansion is His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ ما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ — "Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it...’" until He said: إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرٌ لِلْعالَمِينَ — "It is just a reminder for all the nations" (38:86-87). The address, as indicated by the context of the Surah, is to the disbelievers of the Quraysh; the Almighty says that it is a reminder for all the worlds (العالمين), not exclusive to some over others. Since it is for everyone, it would be meaningless for one of them—like the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله—to ask another for a reward for it.

Furthermore, the fact that the call pertains to the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب), specifically the Jews and Christians, is among the necessities of the Quran. Likewise, the conversion to Islam of non-Arab men such as Salman the Persian, Bilal the Abyssinian, and Suhayb the Roman is among the necessities of history.

It has also been said that the meaning of "those around it" (من حولها) refers to the rest of mankind from the inhabitants of all the villages of the entire earth, a view supported by the designation of Mecca as the "Mother of Cities" (أم القرى).

The verse—as you see—defines revelation by its goal, which is the warning of mankind through divine communication (الإلقاء الإلهي), which is prophethood (النبوة). Thus, revelation (الوحي) is a divine communication for the purpose of prophethood and warning.

His saying, the Exalted: وَتُنْذِرَ يَوْمَ الْجَمْعِ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ فَرِيقٌ فِي الْجَنَّةِ وَفَرِيقٌ فِي السَّعِيرِ — "and warn of the Day of Gathering, in which there is no doubt: a part will be in paradise and a part in the Blaze" (42:7). This is coordinated (عطف) to the previous "that you may warn" (لتنذر). It is an instance of coordinating the specific to the general due to its importance, as if it were said: "to warn the people and make them fear Allah, and specifically His wrath on the Day of Gathering."

His saying: "the Day of Gathering" (يَوْمَ الْجَمْعِ) is the second object (مفعول ثان) of the verb "warn" (تُنْذِرَ) and is not an adverb of time (ظرف) for it, which is evident. The Day of Gathering is the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة). The Exalted said: ذلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَجْمُوعٌ لَهُ النَّاسُ — "That is a day on which mankind will be gathered..." until He said: فَمِنْهُمْ شَقِيٌّ وَسَعِيدٌ — "some of them will be wretched and [some] felicitous" (11:103-105).

His saying: فَرِيقٌ فِي الْجَنَّةِ وَفَرِيقٌ فِي السَّعِيرِ — "a part will be in paradise and a part in the Blaze" (42:7) serves as a justification (تعليل) and an answer to a potential objection (دفع الدخل), as if it were asked: "Why warn them of the Day of Gathering?" The reply is: "a part will be in paradise and a part in the Blaze," meaning they will split into two groups: the felicitous (سعيد) who are rewarded and the wretched (شقي) who are punished. Therefore, they should be warned so they may guard against the path of wretchedness and falling into the pit of destruction.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ شاءَ اللهُ لَجَعَلَهُمْ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً — "Had Allah wished, He would have made them one community" (42:8). Since the verse is directed toward explaining the necessity of warning and prophethood due to the separation of people into two groups on the Day of Resurrection, the first thing that comes to mind regarding "making them one community" is the absolute removal of separation and distinction among them by making them all equal in one single state without difference or distinction. In such a case, there would be no need for prophethood or warning.

His saying: وَلكِنْ يُدْخِلُ مَنْ يَشاءُ فِي رَحْمَتِهِ وَالظَّالِمُونَ ما لَهُمْ مِنْ وَلِيٍّ وَلا نَصِيرٍ — "but He admits whomever He wishes into His mercy, and the wrongdoers do not have any guardian or helper" (42:8). This is a corrective statement (استدراك) explaining that His custom (سنة), the Exalted, has proceeded upon differentiation, and He did not will to make them one community. This is indicated by His saying: "He admits whomever He wishes" (يُدْخِلُ مَنْ يَشاءُ), which denotes continuity (الاستمرار), rather than saying "but He admitted" or similar past-tense forms.

In the verse, "whomever He wishes" (مَنْ يَشاءُ) is contrasted with "the wrongdoers" (الظَّالِمُونَ). Thus, those whom He wishes are the non-wrongdoers. The "wrongdoers" (الظالمين) on the Day of Resurrection have been explained by His saying: فَأَذَّنَ مُؤَذِّنٌ بَيْنَهُمْ أَنْ لَعْنَةُ اللهِ عَلَى الظَّالِمِينَ الَّذِينَ يَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَيَبْغُونَها عِوجاً وَهُمْ بِالْآخِرَةِ كافِرُونَ — "Thereupon a caller will announce in their midst, ‘May Allah’s curse be on the wrongdoers! —those who bar [others] from the way of Allah and seek to make it crooked, and who disbelieve in the Hereafter’" (7:44-45). They are the obstinate deniers of the Return (المعاد).

A contrast is also made between "admission into mercy" and the "negation of a guardian and helper." Those admitted into His mercy are those whose Guardian is Allah, while those who have no guardian or helper are those whom Allah does not admit into His mercy. Furthermore, mercy (الرحمة) is Paradise (الجنة), and the absence of guardianship and help necessitates the Blaze (السعير).

The overall meaning of the verse is that Allah, the Glorified, only ordained prophethood and warning—which stem from revelation—because of the separation into two groups that will befall them on the Day of Resurrection, so that they might guard against entering the group of the Blaze.

Had Allah willed, He would have made them one community, so their condition would be equal and they would not split into two groups on the Day of Resurrection. In that case, there would be nothing necessitating prophethood and warning, and thus no revelation. However, He, the Exalted, did not will that; rather, His custom proceeded such that He takes charge of the affairs of one group—the non-wrongdoers—and admits them into Paradise and His mercy. He does not take charge of the affairs of others—the wrongdoers—so they have no guardian or helper, and they end up in the Blaze with no escape from the Fire.

It follows from what has preceded that the intent of "making them one community" is to equalize them by either admitting everyone into Paradise or everyone into the Blaze. That is, He, the Exalted, is not compelled to admit the felicitous into Paradise and the wretched into the Fire; had He not willed it, He would not have done so. But He willed to differentiate between the two groups, and His custom proceeded upon this, and He promised it, and He does not break His promise. Nevertheless, His absolute power (القدرة المطلقة) remains as it is, neither stripped away nor changed. Thus, His saying: "and warn of the Day of Gathering, in which there is no doubt" until the end of the two verses, carries the same meaning as His saying in Surah Hud: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً لِمَنْ خافَ عَذابَ الْآخِرَةِ ذلِكَ يَوْمٌ مَجْمُوعٌ لَهُ النَّاسُ — "There is indeed a sign in that for those who fear the punishment of the Hereafter. That is a day on which mankind will be gathered..." (11:103) through the next seven verses; refer to them and reflect.

It has been said that the intent of "making them one community" is making them all believers who enter Paradise. Al-Zamakhshari said in al-Kashshaf: "The meaning is: Had your Lord willed a will of power (مشيئة قدرة), He would have coerced them all into faith. But He willed a will of wisdom (مشيئة حكمة), so He tasked them and based their affair on what they choose, so that He may admit the believers into His mercy—and they are the ones intended by 'whomever He wishes'; do you not see them placed in opposition to the wrongdoers?—and leave the wrongdoers without a guardian or helper in His punishment."

He argued for the meaning he chose using His saying, the Exalted: وَلَوْ شِئْنا لَآتَيْنا كُلَّ نَفْسٍ هُداها — "Had We wished, We would have given every soul its guidance" (32:13) and His saying: وَلَوْ شاءَ رَبُّكَ لَآمَنَ مَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ كُلُّهُمْ جَمِيعاً — "Had your Lord wished, all those who are on earth would have believed together" (10:99). The proof that the meaning is compulsion (الإلجاء) toward faith is His saying: أَفَأَنْتَ تُكْرِهُ النَّاسَ حَتَّى يَكُونُوا مُؤْمِنِينَ — "Would you then force people until they become believers?" (10:99).

The critique of this is that the verses—as you have seen—are directed toward defining revelation by its goal, and that the separation of people on the Day of Gathering into two groups is a cause that necessitates the existence of prophethood and warning through revelation. His saying: "Had Allah wished, He would have made them one community" is directed toward explaining that He, the Exalted, is not forced into this nor compelled by it; rather, He has the choice not to do it. This meaning is fulfilled simply by not making them split into two groups, but rather one community regardless of what they are. As for them being specifically one believing group, there is no requirement for that here.

As for the two verses he used as evidence, their context is different from the context of the verse under discussion, and their intent is something other than the forced faith he mentioned, as has been discussed previously in this book.

It has also been said that what is most appropriate to the context is their unification in disbelieving (الكفر), by intending that He make them one disbelieving community, as in His saying: كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللهُ النَّبِيِّينَ — "Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent the prophets..." (2:213). Thus, the meaning would be: Had Allah willed, He would have made them one community united in disbelief by not sending them a messenger to warn them, so they would remain as they are in disbelief. "But He admits whomever He wishes into His mercy," meaning that is His way: He sends to all someone to warn them, and those who are affected by it are moved; Allah grants them success in faith and obedience in this world and admits them into His mercy in the Hereafter. Others are not affected by it, and they are the wrongdoers; they live in this world as disbelievers and end up in the Blaze in the Hereafter with no guardian or helper.

The critique of this is, firstly: the meaning of people being "one community" in the verse used for comparison is not their agreement upon disbelief, but rather their lack of difference in matters pertaining to livelihood, as preceded in the commentary of that verse. Even if that were granted, it would lead to a clear contradiction between the verse being compared and the one it is compared to, because the former indicates separation and lack of unity, while the latter indicates the establishment of unity and lack of separation.

If it were answered that the verse used for comparison indicates people being one community according to their nature (الطبع) rather than in actuality (الفعلية), so there is no contradiction between the two verses, this would be rejected because it contradicts the verses indicating that man is a believer according to his original primordial nature (الفطرة), such as His saying, the Exalted: وَنَفْسٍ وَما سَوَّاها فَأَلْهَمَها فُجُورَها وَتَقْواها — "by the soul and Him who fashioned it, and inspired it with [discernment between] its virtues and vices" (91:7-8).

Secondly: this interpretation removes His saying: "but He admits whomever He wishes into His mercy" from its contrast with His saying: "and the wrongdoers..." etc., without evidence, and then burdens itself with assuming what would provide its meaning to preserve the contrast required by the speech.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمِ اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِهِ أَوْلِياءَ فَاللهُ هُوَ الْوَلِيُ — until His saying — فَحُكْمُهُ إِلَى اللهِ — "Have they taken guardians besides Him? But it is Allah who is the Guardian... its judgment rests with Allah" (42:9-10). The particle "or" (أَمِ) expresses disapproval (الإنكار), as mentioned by al-Zamakhshari. Since the previous verse established that Allah, the Glorified, takes charge of the affairs of the believers specifically and admits them into His mercy, and that the wrongdoers are the obstinate disbelievers who have no guardian, this verse addresses their taking of guardians whom they follow and worship besides Him. It was obligatory for them to take Allah as their Guardian, to follow Him and worship Him. Thus, He denounced them for that and argued for the necessity of taking Him as the Guardian with proof after proof, which is His saying: "But it is Allah who is the Guardian" (فَاللهُ هُوَ الْوَلِيُ) etc.

So His saying: "But it is Allah who is the Guardian" is the justification for the previous denunciation of their taking guardians besides Him, and it serves as a proof for the necessity of taking Him as the Guardian. The sentence—(فَاللهُ هُوَ الْوَلِيُ)—denotes the restriction (حصر) of guardianship to Allah. The proof for the basis of His guardianship and its restriction to Him has already been clarified from His saying in the previous verses: "the All-mighty, the All-wise. To Him belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth, and He is the All-exalted, the All-supreme," as we pointed out in the commentary of those verses.

The meaning is: He, the Exalted, is a Guardian in whom guardianship is restricted; thus, it is mandatory for whoever takes a guardian to take Him as the Guardian and not bypass Him for another, since there is no guardian besides Him.

His saying: وَهُوَ يُحْيِ الْمَوْتى — "and He gives life to the dead" (42:9) is a second proof for the necessity of taking Him, the Exalted, alone as the Guardian. Its summary is that the primary purpose of taking a guardian and following him in servitude is to escape the punishment of the Blaze and attain Paradise on the Day of Resurrection. The one who rewards and punishes on the Day of Resurrection is Allah, who gives life to the dead, gathers them, and recompenses them for their deeds. Therefore, He is the one who must be taken as the Guardian, rather than their guardians who are dead, not alive, and do not perceive when they will be resurrected.

His saying: وَهُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ — "and He has power over all things" (42:9) is a third proof for the necessity of taking Him, the Exalted, as the Guardian instead of others. Its summary is that it is necessary in the matter of guardianship that the guardian has power over the affairs and matters of the one he takes charge of. Allah, the Glorified, has power over all things, and no one else has power except to the extent that Allah has empowered them. He is the Owner of what He has given them to own, and the One powerful over what He has empowered them to do. Thus, He is the Guardian; there is no guardian besides Him, the Exalted and Sanctified.

His saying: وَمَا اخْتَلَفْتُمْ فِيهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ فَحُكْمُهُ إِلَى اللهِ — "Whatever you may differ about, its judgment rests with Allah" (42:10) is a fourth proof for His being the Guardian, with no guardian besides Him. The judgment (حكم) of a judge between those who differ is his deciding and establishing the truth that is unstable between them due to their disagreement in affirmation and negation. Difference (الاختلاف) might be in belief (عقيدة), such as the difference over whether the deity is one or many, or it might be in action (عمل) or what pertains to it, such as differences in matters of livelihood and affairs of life. Thus, judgment (الحكم) is synonymous with decree (القضاء) in its external application (مصداقاً), even if they differ in conceptual meaning (مفهوماً).

Furthermore, judgment and decree are only completed if the judge possesses them by some form of ownership (ملك) and authority (ولاية), even if it is by the parties in disagreement granting him that ownership. For example, when two disputants refer to a third party and take him as an arbiter (حكماً) to judge between them and they submit to what he judges, they have granted him ownership of the judgment according to his view and have given him their acceptance and submission; thus, he is their guardian in that matter.

Allah, the Glorified, is the Owner of everything; there is no owner besides Him because everything, in its existence and the effects of its existence, is sustained by Him, the Exalted. Therefore, to Him belongs the judgment and the decree in truth. The Exalted said: كُلُّ شَيْءٍ هالِكٌ إِلَّا وَجْهَهُ لَهُ الْحُكْمُ وَإِلَيْهِ تُرْجَعُونَ — "Everything is to perish except His Face. All judgement belongs to Him, and to Him you will be brought back" (28:88). And He said: إِنَّ اللهَ يَحْكُمُ ما يُرِيدُ — "Indeed Allah judges as He pleases" (5:2). And He said: الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكَ — "The truth is from your Lord" (3:60).

His judgment, the Exalted, is either ontological (تكويني) — His actualizing and establishing effects in the face of the gathered causes that compete for them, by prioritizing what we call the complete cause over others. The Exalted said, narrating from Jacob (peace be upon him): إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ — "Judgement belongs only to Allah. In Him I have put my trust" (12:67). Or it is legislative (تشريعي) — such as the obligations placed in the divine religion pertaining to belief and action. The Exalted said: إِنِ الْحُكْمُ إِلَّا لِلَّهِ أَمَرَ أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا إِيَّاهُ ذلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ — "Judgement belongs only to Allah. He has commanded you to worship none except Him. That is the upright religion" (12:40).

There is a third type of judgment that can be considered from each of the two previous types in a certain way, which is His judgment, the Exalted, on the Day of Resurrection between His servants in that which they differed. This is His announcing and manifesting the truth on the Day of Resurrection to the gathered people, who will witness it with their own eyes and with certainty. Thus, whoever was with the truth will be felicitous by it and its effects, and whoever was arrogant against it will be wretched by that arrogance and its consequences. The Exalted said: فَاللهُ يَحْكُمُ بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ فِيما كانُوا فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ — "Allah will judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they used to differ" (2:113).

Furthermore, the difference of people in their beliefs and actions is a legislative difference that can only be resolved by legislative judgments and laws. Were it not for difference, no law would exist, as indicated by His saying, the Exalted: كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللهُ النَّبِيِّينَ مُبَشِّرِينَ وَمُنْذِرِينَ وَأَنْزَلَ مَعَهُمُ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ فِيمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ وَمَا اخْتَلَفَ فِيهِ إِلَّا الَّذِينَ أُوتُوهُ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَتْهُمُ الْبَيِّناتُ بَغْياً بَيْنَهُمْ فَهَدَى اللهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لِمَا اخْتَلَفُوا فِيهِ مِنَ الْحَقِّ بِإِذْنِهِ — "Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent the prophets as bearers of good news and as warners, and He sent down with them the Book with the truth, that it may judge between the people concerning that about which they differed. None differed about it except those who had been given it, after the manifest proofs had come to them, out of envy among themselves. Then Allah guided those who believed to the truth of what they differed about by His leave" (2:213).

It has been clarified that legislative judgment belongs to Allah, the Glorified; thus, He is the Guardian in that regard. Therefore, He alone must be taken as the Guardian, so He is worshipped and followed according to the religion He has sent down.

This is the meaning of His saying: "Whatever you may differ about, its judgment rests with Allah." The summary of the proof is that the Guardian who is worshipped and followed must be the one who removes the differences of those he takes charge of, rectifies what has become corrupt in the affairs of their society, and leads them to the happiness of eternal life through the judgment He places upon them, which is the religion. Judgment in that regard belongs to Allah, the Glorified; thus, He is the Guardian who must be taken as the Guardian, and no other.

Others have different interpretations for the verse, i.e., His saying: "Whatever you may differ about, its judgment rests with Allah." It has been said: it is a narration of the words of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to the believers, meaning: whatever the disbelievers from the People of the Book and the polytheists oppose you in, and you and they differ about it regarding a matter of religion, the judgment of that differed-upon matter is delegated to Allah, and He is the one who rewards the truthful among the believers and punishes the practitioners of falsehood; this was mentioned by the author of al-Kashshaf.

It has also been said its meaning is: whatever you differ about and dispute in any litigation, then seek judgment for it from the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and do not prefer any judgment over his judgment, as in His saying, the Exalted: فَإِنْ تَنازَعْتُمْ فِي شَيْءٍ فَرُدُّوهُ إِلَى اللهِ وَالرَّسُولِ — "then, if you fall out with each other concerning anything, refer it to Allah and the Apostle" (4:59).

It has also been said: the meaning is whatever you differ about regarding the interpretation of a verse and it becomes ambiguous to you, then refer for its clarification to the definitive (محكم) part of the Book of Allah and the manifest Sunnah of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

It has also been said: the meaning is whatever you differ about regarding sciences that do not relate to your obligations and for which you have no way to knowledge, then say: "Allah knows best," such as the knowledge of the spirit (الروح). The Exalted said: وَيَسْئَلُونَكَ عَنِ الرُّوحِ قُلِ الرُّوحُ مِنْ أَمْرِ رَبِّي — "They question you concerning the spirit. Say, ‘The spirit is of my Lord’s command’" (17:85). According to all these views, the verse is from the speech of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, either by way of narration or by assuming "Say" (قل) at its beginning.

By reflecting on the context of the verses and then returning to what preceded, you will have no doubt about the invalidity of these views.

His saying, the Exalted: ذلِكُمُ اللهُ رَبِّي عَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْتُ وَإِلَيْهِ أُنِيبُ — "That is Allah, my Lord. In Him I have put my trust, and to Him I turn penitently" (42:10). This is speech narrated from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. The demonstrative "That" (ذلكم) refers to the One for whom proofs were established in the two verses regarding the necessity of taking Him as the Guardian, and He is Allah, the Glorified. The necessity of His guardianship is His Lordship (ربوبيته).

When the proofs were established that He, the Exalted, is the Guardian and there is no guardian besides Him, he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was commanded to announce that He is Allah and that he has taken Him as the Guardian by acknowledging His Lordship, which is the ownership of governance (ملك التدبير). Then he followed that by explicitly stating the effects of the aforementioned taking [of Him as Guardian], which is his saying: "In Him I have put my trust, and to Him I turn penitently."

This is because the guardianship of Lordship pertains to the ontological system (نظام التكوين) through the management of affairs and the organization of causes and effects, such that what is ordained for the governed creature—like man, for example—of existence and survival is determined by it. It also pertains to the legislative system (نظام التشريع), which is the management of human actions by establishing laws and judgments that man observes by applying his actions to them in the course of his life, so they may lead him to the perfection of his happiness.

The necessity of taking Him, the Exalted, as Lord and Guardian from the ontological perspective is to refer the matter of governance to Him by detaching from apparent causes and relying on Him, insofar as He is the unconquerable Cause to whom every cause returns; this is trust (التوكل). From the legislative perspective, it is to return to His judgment in every event man encounters in the course of his life; this is penitent turning (الإنابة). Thus, his saying: "In Him I have put my trust, and to Him I turn penitently," meaning "I return in all my affairs," is an explicit statement of referring the matter to Him ontologically and legislatively.

His saying, the Exalted: فاطِرُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "The Originator of the heavens and the earth..." (42:11) until the end of the verse. When he explicitly stated that He, the Exalted, is his Lord because of the established proofs that He alone is the Guardian, he followed that by establishing the proof in this verse and the one after it for His Lordship, the Exalted, alone.

The summary of the proof is: He, the Exalted, is the Bringer-into-being of things and their Originator (فاطرها) by bringing them out from the concealment of non-existence into existence. He has made for you mates from yourselves, thereby multiplying you, and He made mates from the cattle, thereby multiplying them, so that you may benefit from them; this is creation and governance. He is All-hearing (سميع) of the needs His creation asks of Him, so He fulfills for each the need they deserve. He is All-seeing (بصير) of the deeds His creation performs, so He recompenses them for what they have done. He is the one who owns the keys to the treasuries of the heavens and the earth, in which are stored the properties of their existence and its effects, from which the witnessed system is composed by their appearance. He is the one who provides for the recipients of provision, expanding their provision or restricting it based on His knowledge of that. All of this is from governance (التدبير); thus, He is the Lord who governs all affairs.

So His saying: "The Originator of the heavens and the earth" (فاطِرُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ) means their Bringer-into-being from the concealment of non-existence by way of origination (إبداع).

His saying: جَعَلَ لَكُمْ مِنْ أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَزْواجاً — "He has made for you mates from yourselves" (42:11). This is by the creation of male and female, through whose pairing the matter of procreation and reproduction is completed and individuals are multiplied. وَمِنَ الْأَنْعامِ أَزْواجاً — "and mates from the cattle" (42:11), meaning He also made mates from the cattle. يَذْرَؤُكُمْ فِيهِ — "He multiplies you therewith" (42:11), meaning He increases your numbers through this making. The address in "He multiplies you" (يَذْرَؤُكُمْ) is to both humans and cattle, giving preponderance (تغليب) to the side of rational beings over others, as mentioned by al-Zamakhshari.

His saying: لَيْسَ كَمِثْلِهِ شَيْءٌ — "Nothing is like Him" (42:11), meaning there is nothing like Him. The "ka" (ك) is extra for emphasis, and it has many parallels in the speech of the Arabs.

His saying: وَهُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْبَصِيرُ — "and He is the All-hearing, the All-seeing" (42:11), meaning the All-hearing of the requests His creation raises to Him, and the All-seeing of the deeds of His creation. The Exalted said: يَسْئَلُهُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "Everyone in the heavens and the earth asks Him" (55:29). And He said: وَآتاكُمْ مِنْ كُلِّ ما سَأَلْتُمُوهُ — "and He gave you all that you had asked Him" (14:34). And He said: وَاللهُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ بَصِيرٌ — "and Allah is watchful of what you do" (57:4).

His saying, the Exalted: لَهُ مَقالِيدُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — "To Him belong the keys of the heavens and the earth" (42:12) until the end of the verse. The keys (المقاليد) are the openers (المفاتيح). In affirming the keys for the heavens and the earth, there is an indication that they are treasuries for the events and existential effects that appear in the universe.

His saying: يَبْسُطُ الرِّزْقَ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ وَيَقْدِرُ — "He expands the provision for whomever He wishes, and tightens it" (42:12). Expanding (بسط) provision is its enlargement, and tightening (قدر) it is its restriction. Provision (الرزق) is everything that sustains survival and fulfills a need among the needs of existence in its continuity.

The conclusion of the speech with His saying: إِنَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ عَلِيمٌ — "Indeed He has knowledge of all things" (42:12) is to indicate that provision and its variation in its instances—through expansion and tightening—is not by way of haphazardness or ignorance, but rather from His knowledge, the Exalted, of everything. Thus, the provision of every recipient is based on His knowledge of what the recipient requires according to their state, the provision according to its state, and the external conditions and circumstances surrounding them both. This is wisdom (الحكمة); thus, He expands and tightens with wisdom.

Ayahs

۞ شَرَعَ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلدِّينِ مَا وَصَّىٰ بِهِۦ نُوحًا وَٱلَّذِىٓ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ وَمَا وَصَّيْنَا بِهِۦٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰٓ ۖ أَنْ أَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلدِّينَ وَلَا تَتَفَرَّقُوا۟ فِيهِ ۚ كَبُرَ عَلَى ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ مَا تَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَيْهِ ۚ ٱللَّهُ يَجْتَبِىٓ إِلَيْهِ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَهْدِىٓ إِلَيْهِ مَن يُنِيبُ (١٣)
He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have [also] revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus, declaring, ‘Maintain the religion, and do not be divided in it.’ Hard on the polytheists is that to which you summon them. Allah chooses for it whomever He wishes, and He guides to it whomever returns penitently [to Him]. (13)
وَمَا تَفَرَّقُوٓا۟ إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْعِلْمُ بَغْيًۢا بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِن رَّبِّكَ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى لَّقُضِىَ بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ أُورِثُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ لَفِى شَكٍّ مِّنْهُ مُرِيبٍ (١٤)
They did not divide [into sects] except after the knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves; and were it not for a prior decree of your Lord [granting them reprieve] until a specified time, decision would have been made between them. Indeed those who were made heirs to the Book after them are in grave doubt concerning it. (14)
فَلِذَٰلِكَ فَٱدْعُ ۖ وَٱسْتَقِمْ كَمَآ أُمِرْتَ ۖ وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ ۖ وَقُلْ ءَامَنتُ بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ مِن كِتَـٰبٍ ۖ وَأُمِرْتُ لِأَعْدِلَ بَيْنَكُمُ ۖ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّنَا وَرَبُّكُمْ ۖ لَنَآ أَعْمَـٰلُنَا وَلَكُمْ أَعْمَـٰلُكُمْ ۖ لَا حُجَّةَ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَكُمُ ۖ ٱللَّهُ يَجْمَعُ بَيْنَنَا ۖ وَإِلَيْهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (١٥)
So summon to this [unity of religion], and be steadfast, just as you have been commanded, and do not follow their desires, and say, ‘I believe in whatever Book Allah has sent down. I have been commanded to do justice among you. Allah is our Lord and your Lord. Our deeds belong to us and your deeds belong to you. There is no quarrel between us and you. Allah will bring us together and toward Him is the destination.’ (15)
وَٱلَّذِينَ يُحَآجُّونَ فِى ٱللَّهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا ٱسْتُجِيبَ لَهُۥ حُجَّتُهُمْ دَاحِضَةٌ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ وَعَلَيْهِمْ غَضَبٌ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ (١٦)
Those who argue concerning Allah, after His call has been answered, their argument stands refuted with their Lord, and upon them shall be [His] wrath, and there is a severe punishment for them. (16)

(Commentary)

A third section of the verses defines divine revelation (الوحي الإلهي) through its effect, which is its core message and the content it encompasses—namely, the single divine religion (الدين الإلهي الواحد) that people must adopt as a way of life (سنة في الحياة) and a path traversed toward their ultimate felicity (سعادتهم).

In these verses, as necessitated by the context, it is explained that the Muhammadan law (الشريعة المحمدية) is the most comprehensive of all revealed laws. It further clarifies that the differences occurring within the religion of Allah, despite its inherent unity, do not stem from the side of heavenly revelation, but rather from the transgression (بغي) of people after knowledge had come to them. Other benefits within the verses will be pointed out during the discussion.

His saying, the Exalted: "He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus" (شَرَعَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الدِّينِ ما وَصَّى بِهِ نُوحاً وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ وَما وَصَّيْنا بِهِ إِبْراهِيمَ وَمُوسى وَعِيسى). It is said: "He prescribed (شرع) the path," meaning He made it a clear and manifest way. Al-Raghib said: "The testament (الوصية) is to charge another with what is to be done, coupled with an exhortation." It is derived from their saying: "A joined land (أرض واصية)," referring to land with continuous vegetation. It is said: awsahu (أوصاه) and wassahu (وصاه) [he enjoined him]. End quote. In its meaning, there is an intimation of importance; for not every matter is enjoined as a testament (يوصى به), but rather that which the testator (الموصي) deems significant and worthy of concern is chosen for it.

Thus, His saying: "He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah" (شَرَعَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الدِّينِ ما وَصَّى بِهِ نُوحاً) means He has clarified and made manifest for you the religion—which is the way of life (سنة الحياة)—that He previously charged and entrusted to Noah with great concern. What is apparent from the context is that the address is to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and his community (أمته), and that what was enjoined upon Noah refers to the law (شريعة) of Noah عليه‌السلام.

His saying: "and which We have revealed to you" (وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ). The apparent contrast between this and the mention of Noah عليه‌السلام suggests that what was revealed to him refers to the specific knowledge (المعارف) and rulings (الأحكام) unique to his law. This was expressed through "revelation" (الإيحاء) rather than "enjoining" (التوصية) because enjoining, as mentioned, pertains specifically to matters of particular importance and concern—namely, the most essential beliefs and deeds. The law of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is comprehensive of everything, great and small, containing both the most essential matters and others. This is in contrast to the laws of others, which were limited to the most essential matters suitable for the state of their nations and consistent with the extent of their readiness.

The shift (الالتفات) in His saying: "and which We have revealed" (وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا) from the third person to the first person plural is to indicate grandeur (العظمة), for the great speak of themselves and their servants and followers.

His saying: "and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus" (وَما وَصَّيْنا بِهِ إِبْراهِيمَ وَمُوسى وَعِيسى) is coordinated with His saying: "which He had enjoined" (ما وَصَّى بِهِ), and it refers to what was prescribed for each of them عليه‌السلام.

The order in which they عليه‌السلام are mentioned follows their chronological sequence: Noah, then Abraham, then Moses, then Jesus عليه‌السلام. The mention of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was placed first for honor and preference, as in His saying, the Exalted: "And when We took from the prophets their covenant, and from you and from Noah, Abraham, Moses and Jesus son of Mary" (وَإِذْ أَخَذْنا مِنَ النَّبِيِّينَ مِيثاقَهُمْ وَمِنْكَ وَمِنْ نُوحٍ وَإِبْراهِيمَ وَمُوسى وَعِيسَى ابْنِ مَرْيَمَ) [Al-Ahzab: 7]. Noah was mentioned first among the others to indicate the antiquity of this law and its long history.

Several points can be derived from the verse:

First: Since the context implies the bestowal of favor (الامتنان), especially considering the end of the verse and the following verse, it indicates that the Muhammadan law is comprehensive (جامعة) of the previous laws. This does not contradict His saying, the Exalted: "For every one of you We have appointed a law and a way" (لِكُلٍّ جَعَلْنا مِنْكُمْ شِرْعَةً وَمِنْهاجاً) [Al-Ma'idah: 48], because a law being specific does not negate its comprehensiveness.

Second: The divine laws (الشرائع الإلهية) attributed to revelation are only the laws of Noah, Abraham, Moses, Jesus, and Muhammad عليه‌السلام. Had there been others, they would have been mentioned to fulfill the requirement of the aforementioned comprehensiveness.

This necessitates, firstly: that there was no law (شريعة) before Noah عليه‌السلام in the sense of laws governing human society and resolving social disputes. Some discussion on this has already passed in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: "Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent the prophets..." (كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللهُ النَّبِيِّينَ) [Al-Baqarah: 213].

Secondly: The prophets sent after Noah followed his law until the mission of Abraham, and thereafter followed Abraham's law until the mission of Moses, and so on.

Third: The prophets who were possessors of laws (أصحاب الشرائع) and the "Possessors of Resolve" (أولو العزم) are these five mentioned in the verse. Had there been others with them, they would have been mentioned. These are the masters of the prophets, and their precedence is also indicated by His saying: "And when We took from the prophets their covenant, and from you and from Noah, Abraham, Moses and Jesus son of Mary" (وَإِذْ أَخَذْنا مِنَ النَّبِيِّينَ مِيثاقَهُمْ وَمِنْكَ وَمِنْ نُوحٍ وَإِبْراهِيمَ وَمُوسى وَعِيسَى ابْنِ مَرْيَمَ) [Al-Ahzab: 7].

His saying: "to maintain the religion and not to be divided" (أَنْ أَقِيمُوا الدِّينَ وَلا تَتَفَرَّقُوا). The word "that" (أَنْ) is explanatory (تفسيرية). Maintaining the religion (إقامة الدين) means preserving it through following and practice. The definite article in "the religion" (الدين) is for reference (للعهد), meaning: maintain this religion prescribed for you. Not being divided therein means preserving its unity by agreeing upon it and not differing over it.

Since prescribing the religion for them was effectively a command for all of them to follow it and act upon it without disagreement, He explained it as the command to maintain the religion and not be divided therein. The sum of it is that they are all required to maintain the religion collectively and not to be divided or scattered by some maintaining it while others abandon it. Maintaining it consists of believing in all that Allah has revealed and acting upon what is obligatory to act upon.

Thus, all the laws that Allah revealed to His prophets are one religion (دين واحد) that must be maintained without division. As for the heavenly rulings (الأحكام السماوية) that are shared and remain as long as moral obligation (التكليف) remains, the meaning of "maintaining" them is clear. As for the rulings prescribed in some of these laws that were abrogated (المنسوخة) in a subsequent law, the reality of an abrogated ruling is that it was a ruling with a specific duration for a specific group of people at a specific time. Its abrogation (نسخه) means the clarification that its duration has ended, not the appearance of its invalidity. The Exalted said: "and Allah speaks the truth, and He guides to the way" (وَاللهُ يَقُولُ الْحَقَّ وَهُوَ يَهْدِي السَّبِيلَ) [Al-Ahzab: 4].
