Thus, the abrogated ruling is always "truth" (حق), except that it was specific to a particular group at a particular time; they were required to believe in it and act upon it, while others are required only to believe in it without acting upon it. This is the meaning of maintaining it and not being divided therein.

It becomes clear, then, that the command to maintain the religion and not be divided therein in His saying: "to maintain the religion and not to be divided therein" (أَنْ أَقِيمُوا الدِّينَ وَلا تَتَفَرَّقُوا فِيهِ) is absolute (مطلق), encompassing all people in all times.

By this, the error of the view held by a group becomes apparent—that the command to maintain and not divide only includes the rulings shared between the laws, excluding the specific ones, which they claim are varying rulings that differ according to the nations' conditions and interests.

This is because there is no reason to restrict the absolute nature of His saying: "maintain the religion and not be divided therein." If it were as they say, the command to maintain would be restricted to the three principles of religion (أصول الدين): Monotheism (التوحيد), Prophethood (النبوة), and the Resurrection (المعاد). As for other secondary rulings (الأحكام الفرعية), there is hardly a single ruling shared in all its details among all the laws. This is something that the context of His saying: "He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined..." etc., absolutely rejects. The same applies to His saying: "Indeed this community of yours is one community, and I am your Lord, so be wary of Me. But they fragmented their affair among themselves into sects" (وَإِنَّ هذِهِ أُمَّتُكُمْ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً وَأَنَا رَبُّكُمْ فَاتَّقُونِ فَتَقَطَّعُوا أَمْرَهُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ زُبُراً) [Al-Mu'minun: 52-53], and His saying: "Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam, and those who were given the Book did not differ except after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves" (إِنَّ الدِّينَ عِنْدَ اللهِ الْإِسْلامُ وَمَا اخْتَلَفَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ بَغْياً بَيْنَهُمْ) [Al 'Imran: 19].

His saying: "Hard for the polytheists is that to which you summon them" (كَبُرَ عَلَى الْمُشْرِكِينَ ما تَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَيْهِ). What is meant by "that to which you summon them" is the religion of monotheism (دين التوحيد) to which the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was calling, not merely the principle of monotheism alone, as the following verse testifies. Its being "hard" (كبره) for the polytheists refers to their extreme difficulty and aversion to accepting it.

His saying: "Allah chooses for Himself whomever He wishes and guides to Himself whoever returns" (اللهُ يَجْتَبِي إِلَيْهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَيَهْدِي إِلَيْهِ مَنْ يُنِيبُ). Choosing (الاجتباء) is the act of gathering and drawing near. The requirement of the consistency of pronouns is that the second and third pronouns in "to Himself" (إِلَيْهِ) refer back to the same referent as the first. The meaning is: Allah gathers and draws toward the religion of monotheism—which is what you call them to—whomever He wishes of His servants, and He guides toward it whoever returns to Him. Thus, the entirety of His saying: "Hard for the polytheists is that to which you summon them. Allah chooses for Himself whomever He wishes" carries the same meaning as His saying: "He has chosen you and has not placed for you any hardship in religion, the faith of your father, Abraham" (هُوَ اجْتَباكُمْ وَما جَعَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ مِنْ حَرَجٍ مِلَّةَ أَبِيكُمْ إِبْراهِيمَ) [Al-Hajj: 78].

It has been said that the two pronouns refer to Allah Himself, and there is no harm in this, but the previous interpretation is more suitable. In any case, His saying: "Allah chooses for Himself..." to the end of the verse is placed in a position of dispensing with the faith of the arrogant polytheists, similar to His saying, the Exalted: "But if they are disdainful, those who are with your Lord glorify Him night and day, and they do not tire" (فإِنِ اسْتَكْبَرُوا فَالَّذِينَ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ يُسَبِّحُونَ لَهُ بِاللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهارِ وَهُمْ لا يَسْأَمُونَ) [Fussilat: 38].

It has also been said that what is meant by "that to which you summon them" is that which you call them to believe in, which is the Message (الرسالة)—meaning your message has become hard for them. And His saying: "Allah chooses..." etc., would be in the sense of His saying: "Allah knows best where to place His message" (اللهُ أَعْلَمُ حَيْثُ يَجْعَلُ رِسالَتَهُ) [Al-An'am: 124], but this is contrary to the apparent meaning.

His saying, the Exalted: "They did not divide except after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves" (وَما تَفَرَّقُوا إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ بَغْياً بَيْنَهُمْ) to the end of the verse. The pronoun in "they divided" (تَفَرَّقُوا) refers to the people understood from the context. Transgression (البغي) is injustice or envy, and its qualification with "among themselves" (بَيْنَهُمْ) indicates its mutual occurrence. The meaning is: the people for whom the law was prescribed did not divide through their disagreement and abandonment of unity except while their division was stemming—or originating—from after knowledge of the truth had come to them, out of injustice or envy that they practiced among themselves.

This is the disagreement in religion (الاختلاف في الدين) that leads to sects and factions, which Allah, the Glorified, attributes in various places in His speech to transgression (البغي). As for the disagreement that leads to the revelation of law—which is the disagreement in the affairs of life and division in matters of livelihood—it is a matter returning to the variation of human natures in their goals. It is the pretext for the descent of revelation and the prescription of law to resolve it, as indicated by His saying: "Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent the prophets..." (كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللهُ النَّبِيِّينَ) [Al-Baqarah: 213], as previously explained.

His saying: "And were it not for a prior word from your Lord [postponing them] until a stated term, it would have been decided between them" (وَلَوْ لا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِنْ رَبِّكَ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى لَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ). The "word" (الكلمة) refers to something like His saying at the time of Adam's عليه‌السلام descent to the earth: "and for you there is an abode on the earth and a provision for a while" (وَلَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمتاعٌ إِلى حِينٍ) [Al-Baqarah: 36].

The meaning is: Were it not that Allah had decreed for them settlement and enjoyment on the earth until a term He named and designated, it would have been decided between them immediately following their division in His religion and their deviation from His path, and He would have destroyed them as demanded by this grave sin.

The objection of one who says: "Allah has indeed decided and destroyed, as He narrates in the stories of Noah, Hud, and Salih عليه‌السلام, and He has said: 'There is a messenger for every nation: so when their messenger comes, it is decided between them with justice' (وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولٌ فَإِذا جاءَ رَسُولُهمْ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ) [Yunus: 47]"—

This is refuted by the fact that the judgment and destruction narrated by the Exalted occurred only among the nations of the prophets during their own time, among the deniers who rejected them. What we are concerned with here in His saying: "And were it not for a prior word from your Lord..." is regarding their nations after them, which is clear from the context.

His saying: "Indeed those who were made to inherit the Book after them are in a disquieting doubt about it" (وَإِنَّ الَّذِينَ أورِثُوا الْكِتابَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ لَفِي شَكٍّ مِنْهُ مُرِيبٍ). The pronoun in "after them" (مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ) refers to those who divided after knowledge out of envy among themselves—the predecessors (الأسلاف). Those who were made to inherit the Book after them are their successors (أخلافهم). The meaning of the verse is that those who initiated the disagreement and established the division did so while having knowledge of the truth, and they only innovated what they innovated out of envy among themselves. Their successors, who inherited the Book after them, are in a disquieting doubt (شك مريب)—one that causes suspicion—concerning it.

What we have presented regarding the meaning of the verse is what the context provides. Others have many opinions in its interpretation that are of no use to recount; one may refer to their books to become acquainted with them.

His saying, the Exalted: "So summon to that, and be steadfast as you have been commanded, and do not follow their desires" (فَلِذلِكَ فَادْعُ وَاسْتَقِمْ كَما أُمِرْتَ وَلا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْواءَهُمْ) to the end of the verse. This is a derivation (تفريع) from what was mentioned regarding the prescription of one religion for all prophets and their nations, and then the division of their nations into predecessors who differed in religion despite knowledge out of envy, and successors who are doubting and suspicious of the Book they inherited. That is: because He has prescribed for you all that He prescribed for those before you, then summon [to it]; and because of what was mentioned regarding the division of some out of envy and the suspicion of others, then be steadfast as you have been commanded and do not follow their desires.

The "for" (اللام) in His saying: "So for that" (فَلِذلِكَ) is for causation (للتعليل). It has been said that the lam means "to" (إلى), i.e., "To that which has been prescribed for you of religion, summon and be steadfast as you have been commanded." Steadfastness (الاستقامة)—as mentioned by Al-Raghib—is to adhere to the straight path (المنهاج المستقيم). His saying: "and do not follow their desires" (وَلا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْواءَهُمْ) is like an explanation for it.

His saying: "and say, 'I believe in whatever Book Allah has sent down'" (وَقُلْ آمَنْتُ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ مِنْ كِتابٍ) is an equalization between the heavenly books in terms of confirming them and believing in them; these are the books revealed from Allah containing the laws.

His saying: "and I have been commanded to do justice among you" (وَأُمِرْتُ لِأَعْدِلَ بَيْنَكُمُ). It has been said that the lam is extra for emphasis, similar to His saying: "and we have been commanded to submit to the Lord of all the worlds" (وَأُمِرْنا لِنُسْلِمَ لِرَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ) [Al-An'am: 71]. The meaning is: "I have been commanded to do justice among you," i.e., to treat you equally, so I do not prefer the strong over the weak, nor the rich over the poor, nor the great over the small; nor do I favor the white over the black, nor the Arab over the non-Arab, nor a Hashimite or Qurayshite over another. The call is directed to everyone, and people are equal before the divine law.

Thus, His saying: "I believe in whatever Book Allah has sent down" is an equalization between the revealed books in terms of belief in them, and His saying: "and I have been commanded to do justice among you" is an equalization between people in terms of the call and the application of the law he brought.

It has also been said that the lam in "to do justice among you" (لِأَعْدِلَ بَيْنَكُمُ) is for causation, meaning: "I have been commanded with what I have been commanded for the purpose of doing justice among you." Similarly, it has been said that justice (العدل) refers to justice in ruling, or justice in judging between you, or other things. These are remote meanings not supported by the context.

His saying: "Allah is our Lord and your Lord" (اللهُ رَبُّنا وَرَبُّكُمْ) etc., is in the position of providing the reason (التعليل) for the aforementioned equalization between the books and laws in believing in them, and between people in their call and the inclusiveness of the rulings for them. Therefore, the speech was presented with a break (فصل) without a coordinating conjunction.

So His saying: "Allah is our Lord and your Lord" points out that the Lord of all is the one Allah, the Exalted. They do not have many lords such that each could be attached to his own lord and they would excel one another by their lords, or each would restrict himself to believing in the law of his own lord. Rather, Allah is the Lord of all, and they are all His servants, owned by Him and governed by His command. The laws revealed to the prophets are from Him, so there is no reason to believe in some and not others—as the Jews believe in the law of Moses but not those after him, and similarly the Christians in the law of Jesus but not Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. Rather, it is obligatory to believe in every Book revealed from Him because they are all from Him.

His saying: "To us our deeds and to you your deeds" (لَنا أَعْمالُنا وَلَكُمْ أَعْمالُكُمْ) points out that deeds, though they differ in being good or evil and in their recompense of reward or punishment, do not transcend their doer. Every person has what he has earned; no one benefits from the deed of another, nor is anyone harmed by the deed of another. Thus, he has no right to advance a person to benefit from his deed or delay a person to be harmed by his deed. Yes, in deeds there is a hierarchy by which the degrees of the doers differ, but that is for Allah in how He calls His servants to account, not for the people—the Prophet and those below him—who are all owned servants, none of whom possesses anything for another. This is what the Exalted mentioned in the dialogue of Noah عليه‌السلام with his people: "They said, 'Shall we believe in you when it is the lowliest who follow you?' He said, 'What knowledge do I have of what they used to do? Their reckoning is only with my Lord, if you only perceived'" (قالُوا أَنُؤْمِنُ لَكَ وَاتَّبَعَكَ الْأَرْذَلُونَ قالَ وَما عِلْمِي بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ إِنْ حِسابُهُمْ إِلَّا عَلى رَبِّي لَوْ تَشْعُرُونَ) [Al-Shu'ara: 111-113]. Similarly, His saying addressing the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "You are not responsible for any part of their reckoning, nor are they responsible for any part of your reckoning" (ما عَلَيْكَ مِنْ حِسابِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ وَما مِنْ حِسابِكَ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ) [Al-An'am: 52].

His saying: "There is no argument between us and you" (لا حُجَّةَ بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَكُمُ). Perhaps the meaning is that there is no argument (حجة) indicating the precedence of some over others between us, which some could establish over others to prove their superiority.

It is possible that the negation of argument is a metaphor for the negation of its consequence, which is contention (الخصومة). That is: there is no contention between us regarding the variation of degrees, because our Lord is one, and in the fact that we are all His servants, we are one, and every soul has what it has earned. Thus, there is no argument in the matter—meaning no contention for which an argument would be taken up.

From here, it becomes clear that there is no basis for the view of some in interpreting the sentence as: "meaning no disputation or contention because the truth has become manifest, so there remains no need for disputation nor any ground for opposition except for obstinacy and stubbornness." End quote. This is because the speech is directed toward explaining what the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was commanded with regarding himself and his community of the way of equality (سنة التسوية), not to prove any of the principles of knowledge (أصول المعارف) such that "argument" would be interpreted as he interpreted it.

His saying: "Allah will gather us together" (اللهُ يَجْمَعُ بَيْنَنا). The first-person plural pronoun therein refers to the collective of the speaker and the addressed in the previous sentences. The "gathering" refers to Allah's gathering them on the Day of Resurrection for reckoning and recompense, as has been said.

It is not far-fetched that the gathering refers to Allah's gathering them in Lordship (الربوبية); for He is the Lord of all and all are His servants. Thus, His saying: "Allah will gather us together" would be an emphasis of His previous saying: "Allah is our Lord and your Lord," and a preparation and prelude for His saying: "and to Him is the ultimate return" (وَإِلَيْهِ الْمَصِيرُ). The meaning of the two sentences would be that Allah is our Originator because He is the Lord of us all, and to Him is our end because to Him is the ultimate return; so there is no originator for what is between us except Him, exalted is His Name.

The apparent requirement of the context for the reasoning (التعليل) would have been to say: "Allah is my Lord and your Lord, for me is my deed and for you are your deeds, there is no argument between me and you," in alignment with his saying: "I believe" (آمَنْتُ) and "I have been commanded to do justice" (وَأُمِرْتُ لِأَعْدِلَ). However, he shifted from the first-person singular to the first-person plural because his previous saying: "He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah..." etc., and his saying: "Allah chooses for Himself whomever He wishes and guides to Himself whoever returns," indicate that there are people who believe in what the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله believed in, respond to his call, and follow his law.

Thus, the referent of the first-person plural in "our Lord" (رَبُّنا), "to us our deeds" (لَنا أَعْمالُنا), and "between us" (بَيْنَنا) is he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the believers in him. The referent of the addressed in His saying: "and your Lord" (وَرَبُّكُمْ), "your deeds" (أَعْمالُكُمْ), and "between you" (بَيْنَكُمُ) is the rest of the people from the People of the Book and the polytheists. The verse is on the pattern of His saying, the Exalted: "Say, 'O People of the Book, come to a word common between us and you: that we will worship no one but Allah, and that we will not ascribe any partner to Him, and that none of us will take others as lords besides Allah.' But if they turn away, say, 'Be witnesses that we are Muslims'" (قُلْ يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ تَعالَوْا إِلى كَلِمَةٍ سَواءٍ بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَكُمْ أَلَّا نَعْبُدَ إِلَّا اللهَ وَلا نُشْرِكَ بِهِ شَيْئاً وَلا يَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُنا بَعْضاً أَرْباباً مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَقُولُوا اشْهَدُوا بِأَنَّا مُسْلِمُونَ) [Al 'Imran: 64].

His saying, the Exalted: "As for those who argue concerning Allah after He has been acknowledged, their argument is void with their Lord, and upon them shall be wrath, and for them there is a severe punishment" (وَالَّذِينَ يُحَاجُّونَ فِي اللهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما اسْتُجِيبَ لَهُ حُجَّتُهُمْ داحِضَةٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ وَعَلَيْهِمْ غَضَبٌ وَلَهُمْ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ). An argument (الحجة) is the speech intended to prove or invalidate something, from hajj (الحج) meaning purpose. Dahd (الدحض) is invalidity and vanishing.

The meaning—as has been said—is: those who argue concerning Allah, i.e., they argue to negate His Lordship or to invalidate His religion after the people have responded (استجاب) to Him and entered His religion due to the manifestation of the proof and the clarity of the path—their argument is void and vanishing with their Lord, and upon them is wrath from Allah, the Exalted, and for them is a severe punishment.

What is apparent is that the "response" (الاستجابة) to Him refers to the true response, which is acceptance based on knowledge untainted by doubt, to which the sound human primordial nature (الفطرة) is compelled. For religion, with its inherent knowledge, is primordial (فطري); the living primordial nature confirms it and responds to it. The Exalted said: "Only those who listen will respond; as for the dead, Allah will resurrect them" (إِنَّما يَسْتَجِيبُ الَّذِينَ يَسْمَعُونَ وَالْمَوْتى يَبْعَثُهُمُ اللهُ) [Al-An'am: 36]. And He said: "by the soul and Him who fashioned it, and inspired it with [the discernment of] its vices and its godwariness" (وَنَفْسٍ وَما سَوَّاها فَأَلْهَمَها فُجُورَها وَتَقْواها) [Al-Shams: 7-8]. And He said: "So set your face to the religion as a Hanif—the primordial nature of Allah according to which He originated mankind" (فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها) [Al-Rum: 30].

The sum of the verse, according to this, is that those who argue concerning Him, the Exalted, or concerning His religion after the response of the sound primordial nature to it, or after the response of the people through their sound primordial nature to it—their argument is void and vanishing with their Lord, and upon them is wrath from Him, and for them is a severe punishment whose magnitude cannot be measured.

This view is somewhat supported by the context of the previous verses, where it is mentioned that Allah prescribed a religion, enjoined it upon His prophets, and chose for it whomever He wished of His servants. Thus, arguing that Allah has a religion by which He makes His servants worship Him is void. It is possible then that His saying: "It is Allah who has sent down the Book with the truth and the Balance" (اللهُ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ وَالْمِيزانَ) is in the position of providing the reason and an argument that voids their argument; so reflect upon it.

It has been said that the pronoun in "concerning Allah" (فِي اللهِ) refers to the Messenger صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and those who responded are the People of the Book; their response to him was their acknowledgment of the arrival of his descriptions and traits in their books. The meaning would be that their arguing concerning him after their acknowledgment of what they acknowledged is void with their Lord.

It has also been said that the pronoun refers to him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the one who responded is Allah, the Exalted, as He responded to his prayer against the leaders of the Quraysh and killed them on the day of Badr, and his prayer against the people of Mecca so He afflicted them with drought and hardship, and his prayer for the oppressed until Allah delivered them from the hands of the Quraysh, among other miracles. Both meanings are remote from the context.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Ruh al-Ma'ani, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "As for those who argue concerning Allah..." the verse: Narrated from Ibn Abbas and Mujahid: It was revealed concerning a group of the Children of Israel—who intended to turn people away from Islam and mislead them—so they said: "Our Book is before your Book and our Prophet is before your Prophet—so our religion is better than your religion." In another narration: instead of "so our religion" etc., "so we are more entitled to Allah than you."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from 'Ikrimah who said: When "When the help of Allah comes and the victory" (إِذا جاءَ نَصْرُ اللهِ وَالْفَتْحُ) was revealed, the polytheists in Mecca said to the believers among them: "The people have entered the religion of Allah in throngs—so leave from among us—for what reason do you remain among us?" Then was revealed: "As for those who argue concerning Allah after He has been acknowledged..." the verse.

I say: The content of the verse does not correspond to the narration, as there is no "arguing" (محاجة) in the story. Similarly, the previous report does not suffice to explain His saying: "after He has been acknowledged" (مِنْ بَعْدِ ما اسْتُجِيبَ لَهُ).

Ayahs

ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ وَٱلْمِيزَانَ ۗ وَمَا يُدْرِيكَ لَعَلَّ ٱلسَّاعَةَ قَرِيبٌ (١٧)
It is Allah who has sent down the Book with the truth and [He has sent down] the Balance. What do you know—maybe the Hour is near! (17)
يَسْتَعْجِلُ بِهَا ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهَا ۖ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مُشْفِقُونَ مِنْهَا وَيَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّهَا ٱلْحَقُّ ۗ أَلَآ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُمَارُونَ فِى ٱلسَّاعَةِ لَفِى ضَلَـٰلٍۭ بَعِيدٍ (١٨)
Those who do not believe in it ask [you] to hasten it, but those who have faith are apprehensive of it, and know that it is true. Indeed those who are in doubt about the Hour are in extreme error! (18)
ٱللَّهُ لَطِيفٌۢ بِعِبَادِهِۦ يَرْزُقُ مَن يَشَآءُ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلْقَوِىُّ ٱلْعَزِيزُ (١٩)
Allah is all-attentive to His servants. He provides for whomever He wishes, and He is the All-strong, the All-mighty. (19)
مَن كَانَ يُرِيدُ حَرْثَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ نَزِدْ لَهُۥ فِى حَرْثِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن كَانَ يُرِيدُ حَرْثَ ٱلدُّنْيَا نُؤْتِهِۦ مِنْهَا وَمَا لَهُۥ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مِن نَّصِيبٍ (٢٠)
Whoever desires the tillage of the Hereafter, We will enhance for him his tillage, and whoever desires the tillage of the world, We will give it to him, but he will have no share in the Hereafter. (20)
أَمْ لَهُمْ شُرَكَـٰٓؤُا۟ شَرَعُوا۟ لَهُم مِّنَ ٱلدِّينِ مَا لَمْ يَأْذَنۢ بِهِ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةُ ٱلْفَصْلِ لَقُضِىَ بَيْنَهُمْ ۗ وَإِنَّ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٢١)
Do they have deities [besides Allah] who have ordained for them a religion not permitted by Allah? Were it not for a [prior] conclusive word, judgement would have been made between them, and indeed a painful punishment awaits the wrongdoers. (21)
تَرَى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مُشْفِقِينَ مِمَّا كَسَبُوا۟ وَهُوَ وَاقِعٌۢ بِهِمْ ۗ وَٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ فِى رَوْضَاتِ ٱلْجَنَّاتِ ۖ لَهُم مَّا يَشَآءُونَ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَضْلُ ٱلْكَبِيرُ (٢٢)
When it is about to befall them, You will see the wrongdoers fearful because of what they have earned; but those who have faith and do righteous deeds will be in the gardens of paradise: they will have whatever they wish near their Lord. That is the mighty grace. (22)
ذَٰلِكَ ٱلَّذِى يُبَشِّرُ ٱللَّهُ عِبَادَهُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ ۗ قُل لَّآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْرًا إِلَّا ٱلْمَوَدَّةَ فِى ٱلْقُرْبَىٰ ۗ وَمَن يَقْتَرِفْ حَسَنَةً نَّزِدْ لَهُۥ فِيهَا حُسْنًا ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ شَكُورٌ (٢٣)

أَمْ يَقُولُونَ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ كَذِبًا ۖ فَإِن يَشَإِ ٱللَّهُ يَخْتِمْ عَلَىٰ قَلْبِكَ ۗ وَيَمْحُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْبَـٰطِلَ وَيُحِقُّ ٱلْحَقَّ بِكَلِمَـٰتِهِۦٓ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ عَلِيمٌۢ بِذَاتِ ٱلصُّدُورِ (٢٤)
Do they say, ‘He has fabricated a lie against Allah’? If so, should Allah wish He would set a seal on your heart, and Allah will efface the falsehood and confirm the truth with His words. Indeed He knows well what is in the breasts. (24)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى يَقْبَلُ ٱلتَّوْبَةَ عَنْ عِبَادِهِۦ وَيَعْفُوا۟ عَنِ ٱلسَّيِّـَٔاتِ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا تَفْعَلُونَ (٢٥)
It is He who accepts the repentance of His servants, and excuses their misdeeds and knows what you do. (25)
وَيَسْتَجِيبُ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَيَزِيدُهُم مِّن فَضْلِهِۦ ۚ وَٱلْكَـٰفِرُونَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ (٢٦)
He answers [the supplications of] those who have faith and do righteous deeds and enhances them out of His grace. But as for the faithless, there is a severe punishment for them. (26)

(Commentary)

This is the fourth section of the verses, which defines divine revelation (الوحي) by stating that the religion (الدين) sent down through it is a Book (كتاب) prescribed for people and a Balance (الميزان) by which their deeds are weighed, so they may be recompensed accordingly on the Day of Resurrection (يوم القيامة). The good recompense is described as provision (الرزق), and then the discourse digresses into the reward and punishment that await them on the Day of Resurrection. Within these verses is the Verse of Affection (آية المودة) regarding the near of kin (القربى) and what pertains to it.

His saying, the Exalted: اللهُ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ وَالْمِيزانَ — It is Allah who has sent down the Book with the truth and the Balance (42:17), etc. The opening of the previous sections followed the context of verbal sentences reporting on revelation, its purpose, and its effects: كَذلِكَ يُوحِي إِلَيْكَ — Thus does He reveal to you (42:3); وَكَذلِكَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ — Thus have We revealed to you (42:7); شَرَعَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الدِّينِ — He has prescribed for you the religion (42:13). However, the context changes at the opening of this section, employing a nominal sentence (الجملة الاسمية) that includes the description of the Exalted as the One who sent down the Book and the Balance: اللهُ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ الْكِتابَ — It is Allah who has sent down the Book. The implication of this is the definition of revelation (الوحي) as the descent of the Book and the Balance through it.

Perhaps the reason for this is what preceded in the previous verse regarding the mention of disputing about Allah: وَالَّذِينَ يُحَاجُّونَ فِي اللهِ — As for those who dispute concerning Allah (42:16). This necessitated that the Exalted define Himself to those disputing about Him as the One who sent down the Book with the truth and the Balance. Consequently, this defines revelation by its effect, as you have seen.

In any case, what is meant by the Book (الكتاب) is the revelation containing the law (الشريعة) and the religion (الدين) that governs human society. It has already been mentioned in the commentary (تفسير) on His saying, the Exalted: كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً — Mankind were but a single community (2:213), that this meaning is what is intended by "the Book" in the Book. Its being sent down "with the truth" (بالحق) means its descent is accompanied by the truth, untainted by any satanic or psychological discord.

The Balance (الميزان) is that by which things are weighed and measured. What is meant by it, based on the context of the end of the verse and the subsequent verses, is the religion (الدين) contained within the Book, as it is the standard by which beliefs and deeds are weighed. Thus, they are called to account based on it, and the recompense on the Day of Resurrection is meted out according to it. Therefore, the Balance is the religion in its fundamental principles (أصوله) and its branches (فروعه). This is supported by His saying, the Exalted:

لَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا رُسُلَنا بِالْبَيِّناتِ وَأَنْزَلْنا مَعَهُمُ الْكِتابَ وَالْمِيزانَ — Certainly We sent Our apostles with manifest proofs, and We sent down with them the Book and the Balance (57:25), according to the apparent meaning of His saying: مَعَهُمُ — with them.

It has been said that it refers to justice (العدل), and justice is called a balance because the balance is an instrument of equity and equalization among people, and justice is likewise. This is supported by the prior mention of justice in His saying: وَأُمِرْتُ لِأَعْدِلَ بَيْنَكُمُ — and I have been commanded to do justice between you (42:15).

However, there is no evidence for this in the wording itself. It was previously mentioned that the meaning of justice in لِأَعْدِلَ — to do justice — is equality among people in the delivery of the message and the application of the ruling, rather than the justice of a ruler or a judge.

It has also been said that it refers to the well-known balance used for measuring weights. This is as you see [unlikely].

It has also been said that it refers to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). This can be reconciled with what we previously mentioned, as the Prophet is the perfect manifestation (مصداق كامل) and the supreme example (مثل أعلى) of the religion in its principles and branches. Every individual of his community possesses a degree of religious weight (الزنة الدينية) in proportion to their resemblance and similarity to him. However, this view does not align as closely with the verse from Surah al-Hadid mentioned earlier.

His saying: وَما يُدْرِيكَ لَعَلَّ السَّاعَةَ قَرِيبٌ — And what do you know? Maybe the Hour is near (42:17). Since the Balance implies accounting and recompense on the Day of Resurrection, the discourse shifts to it, warning them of the terrors they will face therein and giving glad tidings of what has been prepared for the righteous.

Informing (الادراء) means notifying. What is meant by the Hour (الساعة) — as has been said — is its arrival, which is why the predicate is masculine (قريب). The meaning is: What makes you know? Perhaps the arrival of the Hour is near. The address is to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in his capacity as a listener, thus including everyone capable of hearing, making the warning and intimidation general.

His saying, the Exalted: يَسْتَعْجِلُ بِهَا الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِها وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا مُشْفِقُونَ مِنْها — Those who do not believe in it seek to hasten it, but those who have faith are apprehensive of it (42:18), etc. Their seeking to hasten it refers to a seeking characterized by mockery and derision. The Quran repeatedly records their saying: مَتى هذَا الْوَعْدُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ — When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful? (10:48).

Apprehension (الإشفاق) is a type of fear. Al-Raghib said: "Apprehension is care (عناية) mixed with fear, because the apprehensive one loves the one they are apprehensive for and fears what might befall them." The Exalted said: وَهُمْ مِنَ السَّاعَةِ مُشْفِقُونَ — and they are apprehensive of the Hour (21:49). When it is followed by the preposition min (من), the meaning of fear is more apparent, and when followed by fi (في), the meaning of care is more apparent. The Exalted said: إِنَّا كُنَّا قَبْلُ فِي أَهْلِنا مُشْفِقِينَ — 'Before this, we used to be apprehensive among our families' (52:26); and مُشْفِقُونَ مِنْها — apprehensive of it. [End of quote].

His saying: أَلا إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يُمارُونَ فِي السَّاعَةِ لَفِي ضَلالٍ بَعِيدٍ — Behold! Indeed those who dispute concerning the Hour are in remote error (42:18). Disputing (المماراة) is persistence in argumentation. What is meant is their insistence on denying it through argumentation. They are in "remote error" because they have missed the path of life that hits upon the most important thing conceivable for a human being. They imagined it to be a severed, fleeting life in which they indulge in worldly desires, whereas it is an eternal, everlasting life for which they must gather provisions from their world for their hereafter. However, they strayed from the path of rectitude (الرشد) and fell into the path of error (الغي).

His saying, the Exalted: اللهُ لَطِيفٌ بِعِبادِهِ يَرْزُقُ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَهُوَ الْقَوِيُّ الْعَزِيزُ — Allah is all-attentive to His servants. He provides for whomever He wishes, and He is the All-strong, the All-mighty (42:19). The meaning of subtlety (اللطف) — the quality of being all-attentive and subtle — involves an element of gentleness (الرفق) and ease of action, as well as an element of precision (الدقة) regarding that upon which the action falls. When gentleness and precision are complete, and the agent acts with gentleness and ease, and his action falls upon precise matters, he is "subtle" (لطيف), like air that penetrates the openings of bodies with gentleness and ease, touching the minute parts of their interior. When material specificities are stripped from this meaning, it is correct to describe Allah, the Glorified, with it. For He, the Exalted, reaches the minute details of matters through His encompassment and knowledge, and He acts within them as He wills with gentleness; thus, He is Subtle (لطيف).

Provision (الرزق) in the verse is arranged upon His being Subtle with His servants, All-strong, and All-mighty, to indicate that through His subtlety, no one whom He wishes to provide for is hidden from Him, nor does anyone disobey Him; through His strength over them, He is never incapable; and through His might, no preventer can stop Him.

What is meant by provision (الرزق) is that which encompasses the gift of religion (الدين) with which He clothes whomever He wishes of His servants, as evidenced by the following verse. For this reason, the discourse regarding it was joined to His saying: اللهُ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ وَالْمِيزانَ — It is Allah who has sent down the Book with the truth and the Balance.

His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ حَرْثَ الْآخِرَةِ نَزِدْ لَهُ فِي حَرْثِهِ — Whoever desires the tillage of the Hereafter, We will enhance his tillage for him (42:20), etc. Tillage (الحرث) is cultivation. What is meant by it is the result of the deeds given to man in the Hereafter, used as a metaphor (استعارة); as if righteous deeds (الأعمال الصالحة) are seeds and what they produce in the Hereafter is tillage.

The meaning of increasing his tillage for him is the multiplication and doubling of his reward. The Exalted said: مَنْ جاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ عَشْرُ أَمْثالِها — Whoever brings a virtue shall receive ten times its like (6:160); and He said: وَاللهُ يُضاعِفُ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ — and Allah multiplies for whomever He wishes (2:261).

His saying: وَمَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ حَرْثَ الدُّنْيا نُؤْتِهِ مِنْها وَما لَهُ فِي الْآخِرَةِ مِنْ نَصِيبٍ — and whoever desires the tillage of the world, We will give him [something] of it, and he shall have no share in the Hereafter (42:20). That is, whoever desires worldly results by working for the world and wanting the result of his work therein rather than the Hereafter, We give him of the world, and he has no share in the Hereafter. The expression "desiring the tillage" (إرادة الحرث) points to the condition of action (العمل) for what one desires of the world and the Hereafter, as the Exalted said: وَأَنْ لَيْسَ لِلْإِنْسانِ إِلَّا ما سَعى — and that nothing belongs to man except what he strives for (53:39).

He left vague what He gives of the world when He said: نُؤْتِهِ مِنْها — We will give him [something] of it, indicating that the matter is subject to the divine will (المشية الإلهية); He may expand provision or He may restrict it, as the Exalted said: مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ الْعاجِلَةَ عَجَّلْنا لَهُ فِيها ما نَشاءُ لِمَنْ نُرِيدُ — Whoever desires this transitory life, We expedite for him therein whatever We wish, for whomever We desire (17:18).

The shift (الالتفات) from the third person to the first person plural in His saying نَزِدْ لَهُ — We will enhance for him — and نُؤْتِهِ مِنْها — We will give him of it — is to indicate the grandeur (العظمة) suggested by His saying: وَهُوَ الْقَوِيُّ الْعَزِيزُ — and He is the All-strong, the All-mighty.

The summary of the meaning of the two verses is: Allah, the Glorified, is subtle with all His servants, possessing absolute power and absolute might. He provides for His servants according to His will. He has willed that whoever desires the Hereafter and works for it, He will provide for him from it and increase it for him; and whoever desires the world and works for it alone, He will give him of it, and he shall have no share in the Hereafter.

From this, it appears that the first verse is general, encompassing both groups, and "servants" (العباد) refers to both the people of the world and the people of the Hereafter, as does "provision" (الرزق). The second verse serves to detail the ambiguity (الإجمال) in His saying: يَرْزُقُ مَنْ يَشاءُ — He provides for whomever He wishes.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ لَهُمْ شُرَكاءُ شَرَعُوا لَهُمْ مِنَ الدِّينِ ما لَمْ يَأْذَنْ بِهِ اللهُ — Do they have partners who have prescribed for them a religion which Allah has not allowed? (42:21) to the end of the verse. Since He explained that Allah, the Glorified, is the One who sent down the Book with the truth and prescribed for them the religion which is the balance of their deeds, and that through His subtlety, power, and might, He provides for whoever desires the Hereafter and works for it what they desired and more; and that whoever desires the world and forgets the Hereafter has no share in it—He then recorded against those who disbelieve in the Hereafter their lack of a share therein by denying that there is any religion other than what Allah has prescribed that these people could follow so as to be provided for by acting upon it, just as the people of faith in the Hereafter are provided for. For there is no partner to Allah who could prescribe a religion other than what Allah has prescribed without His permission. Thus, there is no religion except for Allah, and no one will be provided with a good provision in the Hereafter except those who believe in it and work for it.

So His saying: أَمْ لَهُمْ شُرَكاءُ — Do they have partners? — etc., is in the position of denial (الإنكار). His saying: وَلَوْ لا كَلِمَةُ الْفَصْلِ لَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ — Had it not been for a decisive word, the matter would have been decided between them (42:21), refers to the word that preceded from the Exalted that they shall live on the earth until a specified term. In this is an emphasis on the magnitude of their crime and disobedience.

His saying: وَإِنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ أَلِيمٌ — Indeed for the wrongdoers there is a painful punishment (42:21), is a threat to them for their wrongdoing, and an indication that they cannot escape the Exalted. If the matter is not decided between them and they are not punished in the world, then for them in the Hereafter is a painful punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: تَرَى الظَّالِمِينَ مُشْفِقِينَ مِمَّا كَسَبُوا وَهُوَ واقِعٌ بِهِمْ — You will see the wrongdoers apprehensive because of what they have earned, and it shall befall them (42:22), etc. The address is to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in his capacity as a listener, thus including everyone whose function is to see. What is meant by "the wrongdoers" (الظالمين) are those who abandon the religion of Allah which He prescribed for His servants and who turn away from the Hour. The meaning is: Observers will see these wrongdoers on the Day of Resurrection fearful of the evil deeds they earned, and it shall befall them; they have no escape from it.

The verse is among those that clearly indicate the embodiment of deeds (تجسم الأعمال). It has been said that there is a suppressed noun (مضاف محذوف) in the speech, and the estimation is "apprehensive of the consequence (وبال) of what they earned," but there is no need for this.

His saying: وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ فِي رَوْضاتِ الْجَنَّاتِ — But those who have faith and do righteous deeds will be in the luxuriant gardens of the gardens (42:22). It is mentioned in al-Majma' that the meadow (الروضة) is green land with beautiful vegetation, and the garden (الجنة) is land surrounded by trees. Thus, the "luxuriant gardens of the gardens" (روضات الجنات) are the tree-filled gardens with green expanses.

His saying: لَهُمْ ما يَشاؤُنَ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ — They shall have whatever they wish with their Lord (42:22). That is, the system of causes (نظام الأسباب) is folded away therein; rather, the sole cause is their will alone. Allah creates for them from His presence whatever they wish. That is the great grace.

His saying: ذلِكَ الَّذِي يُبَشِّرُ اللهُ عِبادَهُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ — Such is the good news that Allah gives to His servants who have faith and do righteous deeds (42:23). This is glad tidings for the righteous believers, and the annexation of "servants" (العباد) is for honor (تشريفية).

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى — Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it except the love of [my] relatives’ (42:23). That which the asking of a reward for is negated is the delivery of the message (تبليغ الرسالة) and the religious call (الدعوة الدينية). Allah has narrated this from several messengers before him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), such as Noah, Hud, Salih, Lot, and Shu'ayb, in what is narrated of each of them addressing his people: وَما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ إِنْ أَجْرِيَ إِلَّا عَلى رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ — I do not ask you any reward for it; my reward lies only with the Lord of all the worlds (26:109 and elsewhere).

This has also been narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) when He said: وَما تَسْئَلُهُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ — You do not ask them any reward for it (12:104). He (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was commanded to address the people with this using various expressions, as He said: قُلْ ما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ — Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it’ (38:86); and He said: قُلْ ما سَأَلْتُكُمْ مِنْ أَجْرٍ فَهُوَ لَكُمْ إِنْ أَجْرِيَ إِلَّا عَلَى اللهِ — Say, ‘Whatever reward I may have asked you is for your own selves; my reward lies only with Allah’ (34:47); and He said: قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا ذِكْرى لِلْعالَمِينَ — Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it. It is only a reminder for all the nations’ (6:90). Thus, He pointed to the reason for the negation: that it is a reminder for all the worlds, not exclusive to some over others such that a reward could be taken for it.

And He said: قُلْ ما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ إِلَّا مَنْ شاءَ أَنْ يَتَّخِذَ إِلى رَبِّهِ سَبِيلاً — Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it, except that anyone who wishes should take the way to his Lord’ (25:57). Its meaning, as passed in the commentary on the verse, is: except that one of you should wish to take a path to his Lord—that is, respond to my call by his own choice—then that is my reward. Meaning, there is nothing there beyond the call; i.e., no reward.

And the Exalted said in this Surah: قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى — Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it except the love of [my] relatives’. Thus, He made the reward for his message the affection for the near of kin. It is certain from the contents of other verses on this subject that this affection is a matter that pertains to responding to the call—either the response in its entirety or the response to a part of it that is of great importance. The apparent meaning of the exception (الاستثناء), in any case, is that it is connected (متصل) to the claim that affection is part of the reward, and there is no need for the forced interpretations some have made to suggest it is a disconnected (منقطع) exception.

As for the meaning of "affection for the near of kin" (المودة في القربى), their interpretations have differed:

It has been said—and attributed to the majority—that the address is to the Quraysh, and the requested reward is their affection for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) due to his kinship with them. This is because they used to deny him and hate him for his opposition to their gods, as mentioned in some reports. So he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) was commanded to ask them: if they do not believe in him, they should at least love him for the sake of his kinship with them and not hate or harm him. Thus, kinship (القربى) is a verbal noun meaning relationship, and the preposition fi (في) denotes causality (السببية).

The problem with this is that the meaning of "reward" (الأجر) is only fulfilled when it is in exchange for a work owned by the giver of the reward, who then gives the worker the equivalent of what he owned in wealth or the like. Asking for a reward from the Quraysh, while they were deniers of him and disbelievers in his call, would only be valid on the assumption of their faith in him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). This is because, on the assumption of their denial and disbelief in his call, they have not taken anything from him to reciprocate with a reward. And on the assumption of faith in him—prophethood being one of the three fundamental principles of religion—hatred is inconceivable such that affection would be made a reward for the message and requested.

In short, the meaning of reward is not realized on the assumption of the disbelief of those asked, nor is the meaning of hatred realized on the assumption of their faith such that they would be asked for affection. This objection holds even if the exception is taken as disconnected (منقطع), for asking them for a reward is only conceivable on the assumption of their faith, and the rectification (الاستدراك) in a disconnected exception is from the sentence with all its constraints. So reflect upon this.

It has also been said: What is meant by "affection for the near of kin" is what preceded, but the address is to the Ansar. It has been said that they brought him wealth to assist him in what befell him, so the verse was revealed and he returned it. He had kinship with them through Salma bint Zayd al-Najjariyyah and through the maternal uncles of his mother Aminah, as has been said.

The problem with this is that the matter of the Ansar's love for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) is clearer than for any doubter to doubt. They were the ones who asked him to migrate to them, provided him a home, and sacrificed themselves, their wealth, and their children for him. They exerted all their effort in supporting him and even in doing good to those who migrated to them from among the believers in him. Allah, the Exalted, praised them with verses like: وَالَّذِينَ تَبَوَّؤُا الدَّارَ وَالْإِيمانَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ يُحِبُّونَ مَنْ هاجَرَ إِلَيْهِمْ وَلا يَجِدُونَ فِي صُدُورِهِمْ حاجَةً مِمَّا أُوتُوا وَيُؤْثِرُونَ عَلى أَنْفُسِهِمْ وَلَوْ كانَ بِهِمْ خَصاصَةٌ — [They are also for] those who were settled in the land and [abided] in faith before them, who love those who migrate to them, and do not find in their breasts any need for what they are given, and prefer [the Immigrants] to themselves, though they may be in need (59:9). This is the extent of their love for those who migrated to them for the sake of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله); so what is one to think of their love for him?

If this was the extent of their love, what is the meaning of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) being commanded to appeal to their affection through his kinship with them—this distant kinship? Furthermore, the Arabs did not give such great importance to kinship through women, and among them was the one who said:

Our sons are the sons of our sons, but our daughters— Their sons are the sons of distant men.

And the one who said:

The mothers of people are but vessels, Depositories, while lineages belong to the fathers.

It was only Islam that included women in kinship and equalized between the children of sons and the children of daughters, as previously discussed.

It has also been said: The address is to the Quraysh, and "affection for the near of kin" is affection due to kinship, except that what is meant is the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) love for them, not the Quraysh's love for him as in the first view. The exception is disconnected, and the summary of the meaning is: I do not ask you a reward for the guidance I call you to, which leads you to the luxuriant gardens of the gardens and eternity therein, nor do I seek from you a recompense; rather, my love for you due to your kinship to me has driven me to guide you to it and point you toward it.

The problem with this is that it does not align with the path Allah, the Glorified, has set for him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in the course of the call and guidance. For He, the Exalted, records against him in many places of His speech that the matter of guiding people belongs to Allah and he has no part in the matter, and that he should not grieve over their disbelief and rejection of his call; his duty is only the delivery of the message. Thus, he was not to be driven to guide someone due to love for a relative, nor to turn away from guiding others due to hatred or dislike. Despite all this, how can it be conceived that Allah would command him by saying: قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ — Say, ‘I do not ask you’ — to inform the disbelievers of Quraysh that he was only driven to call and guide them because of his love for them due to their kinship to him, not for a reward he asks of them for it?

It has also been said: What is meant by "affection for the near of kin" is the affection for one's own relatives, and the address is to the Quraysh or to people in general. The meaning is: I do not ask you a reward for my call except that you love your own relatives.

The problem with this is that affection for relatives in an absolute sense is not something exhorted toward in Islam. The Exalted said: لا تَجِدُ قَوْماً يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللهِ وَالْيَوْمِ الْآخِرِ يُوادُّونَ مَنْ حَادَّ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ وَلَوْ كانُوا آباءَهُمْ أَوْ أَبْناءَهُمْ أَوْ إِخْوانَهُمْ أَوْ عَشِيرَتَهُمْ أُولئِكَ كَتَبَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمُ الْإِيمانَ وَأَيَّدَهُمْ بِرُوحٍ مِنْهُ — You will not find a people believing in Allah and the Last Day loving those who oppose Allah and His Apostle, even though they be their fathers, or their sons, or their brothers, or their clan. It is they in whose hearts He has inscribed faith, and whom He has strengthened with a spirit from Him (58:22). The context of this verse does not allow it to be a specifier (مخصص) or a qualifier (مقيد) for the generality of His saying: إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى — except the love of relatives — or its absolute sense, such that affection for believing relatives would be the reward for the message. Furthermore, this specific affection does not align with addressing the Quraysh or people in general.

Rather, what the context of the verse provides is that what Islam exhorts toward is love for the sake of Allah (الحب في الله) without kinship having any particularity in that. Yes, there is intense concern for the matter of kinship and the womb (الرحم), but it is under the heading of maintaining family ties (صلة الرحم) and giving wealth out of love for Him to the near of kin, not under the heading of "affection for the near of kin." For there is no love except for Allah, exalted is His Name.

There is no way to say that "affection for the near of kin" in the verse is a metonymy (كناية) for maintaining ties with them and doing good to them by giving wealth, as there is nothing in the speech to prevent the intended meaning from being the literal one, which is incompatible with what Islam exhorts toward regarding love for the sake of Allah.

It has also been said: The meaning of kinship (القربى) is seeking nearness to Allah, and "affection for the near of kin" is seeking His affection through obedience and seeking nearness. So the meaning is: I do not ask you a reward for it except that you seek His affection by seeking nearness to Him.

The problem with this is that in His saying: إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى — except the love in nearness — according to this meaning, there is an ambiguity (إبهام) that makes it unsuitable for addressing the polytheists. For the core of its meaning is seeking His affection—or His love—by seeking nearness to Him, and the polytheists do not deny this; rather, they see their worship of idols as a way of seeking His affection by seeking nearness to Him. They are the ones who say, as the Quran narrates from them: ما نَعْبُدُهُمْ إِلَّا لِيُقَرِّبُونا إِلَى اللهِ زُلْفى — ‘We do not worship them except that they may bring us near to Allah’ (39:3); and هؤُلاءِ شُفَعاؤُنا عِنْدَ اللهِ — ‘These are our intercessors with Allah’ (10:18).

Thus, asking for the seeking of affection from Allah by seeking nearness to Him without qualifying it as being through His worship alone, and making that a required reward from those who already believe in a type of seeking affection from Allah by seeking nearness to Him—and addressing them with that despite its ambiguity, while the context is one of him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) purely dedicating himself to calling them to the religion of monotheism (التوحيد) without asking them for anything for himself—is something sound taste does not accept.

Furthermore, the term used in the verse is "affection" (المودة) rather than "seeking affection" (التودد). So the intended meaning of affection would be their love for Allah in seeking nearness to Him. However, the use of "affection" (المودة) for the servants' love for Allah, the Glorified, has not occurred in His speech, although the reverse has occurred, as in His saying: إِنَّ رَبِّي رَحِيمٌ وَدُودٌ — ‘Indeed my Lord is all-merciful, all-affectionate’ (11:90); and His saying: وَهُوَ الْغَفُورُ الْوَدُودُ — and He is the All-forgiving, the All-affectionate (85:14). Perhaps this is because the word "affection" (المودة) suggests consideration for the state of the beloved, attending to them, and looking after them, to the point that some have said—as narrated by al-Raghib—that Allah's affection for His servants is His looking after them.

The previous objection remains even if "affection for the near of kin" is interpreted as people's mutual affection and love for one another in seeking nearness to Allah, such that acts of nearness become causes for affection and love among them. For the polytheists have something similar among themselves according to their beliefs.

It has also been said: What is meant by "affection for the near of kin" is the affection for the relatives of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and they are his progeny (عترته) from his Household (أهل بيته) (peace be upon him). Narrations from the sources of the Sunnis have been reported to this effect, and reports from the sources of the Shi'a are numerous in interpreting the verse as affection and devotion (موالاتهم) to them. This is supported by the mutawatir (متواترة) — successively narrated and undeniably authentic — reports from the sources of both groups regarding the obligation of devotion to the Household (أهل البيت) (peace be upon him) and loving them.

Furthermore, sufficient reflection on the mutawatir reports narrated from both groups from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), which involve directing people in their understanding of the Book of Allah—with its fundamental religious sciences and its branches—and the explanation of its realities to the Household (أهل البيت) (peace be upon him), such as the Hadith of the Two Weighty Things (حديث الثقلين) and the Hadith of the Ark (حديث السفينة) and others, leaves no doubt that the obligation of their affection and making it a reward for the message was only a means to direct people to them in their capacity as the scholarly authority (المرجعية العلمية).

Thus, the affection prescribed as a reward for the message was not something separate from the religious call in terms of its survival and continuity. Therefore, the verse in its significance does not differ from the significance of other verses negating the asking of a reward.

Its meaning boils down to: I do not ask you a reward for it, except that since Allah has made the affection for all believers obligatory upon you—and among them are my relatives—I count your affection for my relatives and consider it a reward for my message. The Exalted said: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ سَيَجْعَلُ لَهُمُ الرَّحْمنُ وُدًّا — Indeed those who have faith and do righteous deeds—the All-beneficent will endear them [to His creation] (19:96); and He said: وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ وَالْمُؤْمِناتُ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلِياءُ بَعْضٍ — But the faithful, men and women, are comrades of one another (9:71).

By this, the invalidity of the objection raised against this view becomes clear: that it does not suit the status of prophethood because of the suspicion it entails, for most seekers of the world do something and ask for a reward that benefits their children and relatives. Also, it is said to contradict His saying, the Exalted: وَما تَسْئَلُهُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ — You do not ask them any reward for it (12:104).

The reason for its invalidity is that the application of "reward" to it and naming it as such is only according to the claim (الدعوى); as for the reality, the significance of the verse does not exceed what the other verses negating the asking of a reward indicate, as you have seen. Whatever benefit is in that returns to them, so there is no ground for suspicion.

Moreover, the verse according to this is Medinan, addressed to the Muslims, and they have no right to suspect their Prophet—who is protected by divine infallibility (عصمة إلهية)—after believing in him and testifying to his infallibility, in what he brings them from their Lord. If it were permissible for them to suspect him in that, and if it were unsuitable for the status of prophethood, it would not be fit to be addressed. Such a thing would then apply to many Quranic addresses, such as the verses indicating the obligation of absolute obedience to him, those indicating that the spoils and booties belong to Allah and His Messenger, those indicating the one-fifth (خمس) for the near of kin, what was permitted for him regarding women, and so on.

Furthermore, the Exalted addressed this suspicion and refuted it in His upcoming saying: أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَرى عَلَى اللهِ كَذِباً فَإِنْ يَشَإِ اللهُ يَخْتِمْ عَلى قَلْبِكَ — Do they say, ‘He has fabricated a lie against Allah’? If Allah should wish, He will set a seal on your heart (42:24), as will follow.

And even if we were to turn the verse away from this meaning by interpreting it otherwise to avoid the mentioned suspicion, what would refute it regarding the countless reports narrated from both groups concerning the obligation of affection for the Household from him (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)?

As for the contradiction of this view with His saying, the Exalted: وَما تَسْئَلُهُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ — You do not ask them any reward for it — its invalidity has become clear from what we mentioned. The verse, by comparing its significance to the verses negating the asking of a reward, is similar to His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ ما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ إِلَّا مَنْ شاءَ أَنْ يَتَّخِذَ إِلى رَبِّهِ سَبِيلاً — Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it, except that anyone who wishes should take the way to his Lord’ (25:57).

The author of al-Kashshaf said after choosing this view: "If you say: Why was it not said: illa mawaddata al-qurba or illa al-mawaddata lil-qurba? And what is the meaning of His saying: إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى?"

I say: They made them a place for affection and a residence for it, like your saying: 'I have affection in (في) the family of so-and-so,' and 'I have in them passion and intense love,' meaning you love them and they are the place and location of your love.

He said: And fi (في) is not a connective (صلة) for affection like the lam (ل) when you say: illa al-mawaddata lil-qurba. Rather, it is related to a suppressed [word] to which the adverbial phrase (الظرف) is related in your saying: 'The wealth is in (في) the bag,' and its estimation is: 'except the affection that is established in the near of kin and firmly rooted in them.'" [End of quote].

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ يَقْتَرِفْ حَسَنَةً نَزِدْ لَهُ فِيها حُسْناً إِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ شَكُورٌ — Whoever performs a good deed, We shall enhance its goodness for him. Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-appreciative (42:23). Performing (الاقتراف) is earning. A good deed (الحسنة) is an act that Allah, the Glorified, is pleased with and rewards. The goodness of an act is its suitability for human happiness and the goal intended by it, just as its badness and ugliness are the opposite. Increasing its goodness is the completion of what was deficient in its aspects and its perfection; among this is the increase in its reward, as the Exalted said: وَلَنَجْزِيَنَّهُمْ أَحْسَنَ الَّذِي كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ — and We will surely reward them by the best of what they used to do (29:7); and He said: لِيَجْزِيَهُمُ اللهُ أَحْسَنَ ما عَمِلُوا وَيَزِيدَهُمْ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ — that Allah may reward them by the best of what they have done, and enhance them out of His grace (24:38).

The meaning is: Whoever earns a good deed, We will increase for him the goodness in that deed—by removing its deficiencies and increasing its reward—for indeed Allah is All-forgiving (غفور), erasing evil deeds, and All-appreciative (شكور), manifesting the beauties of the act from its doer.

It has been said: What is meant by the "good deed" (الحسنة) is the affection for the relatives of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). This is supported by what is in the narrations of the Imams of the Household (أهل البيت) (peace be upon him) that His saying: قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً — Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it’ — until the completion of four verses, was revealed regarding the affection for the relatives of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). This necessitates that the verses are Medinan and that they possess a single context, and that what is meant by the "good deed" in terms of its application to the instance is affection. Accordingly, the reference in His saying: أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَرى — Do they say, ‘He has fabricated...’ — etc., is to some of what the hypocrites uttered out of reluctance to accept it, and among the believers were those who listened to them. And His saying: وَهُوَ الَّذِي يَقْبَلُ التَّوْبَةَ — It is He who accepts the repentance — to the end of the two verses, refers to the repentance of those among them who returned and its acceptance.

In His saying: إِنَّ اللهَ غَفُورٌ شَكُورٌ — Indeed Allah is all-forgiving, all-appreciative — there is a shift (التفات) from the first person to the third person. The reason for this is to point to the cause of being characterized by forgiveness and appreciation, for the meaning is: Allah is All-forgiving and All-appreciative because He is Allah, exalted is His Name.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَرى عَلَى اللهِ كَذِباً — Do they say, ‘He has fabricated a lie against Allah’? (42:24) to the end of the verse. The word am (أَمْ) is disconnected (منقطعة), and the speech is intended for rebuke (التوبيخ), the implication of which is the denial of his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) being a fabricator of a lie against Allah.

His saying: فَإِنْ يَشَإِ اللهُ يَخْتِمْ عَلى قَلْبِكَ — If Allah should wish, He will set a seal on your heart (42:24). Its meaning, according to what the context provides, is that you are not a fabricator of a lie against Allah, for you have no part in the matter such that you could wish for fabrication and bring it forth. Rather, it is revelation from Allah, the Glorified, without you having any hand in it. The matter is subject to His will, the Exalted; if He wished, He would seal your heart and close the door of revelation to you. However, He willed to reveal to you and clarify the truth, and His sunnah (سنة) — the established divine way — has proceeded to erase falsehood and establish the truth by His words.

So His saying: فَإِنْ يَشَإِ اللهُ يَخْتِمْ عَلى قَلْبِكَ — If Allah should wish, He will set a seal on your heart — is a metonymy (كناية) for returning the matter to the will of Allah and a sanctification of the Prophet's (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) station from bringing anything from himself.

This meaning—as you will see—is more suitable for the context based on the interpretation that "the near of kin" refers to the relatives of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), with the rebuke directed at the hypocrites and those with diseased hearts.

They have mentioned other views regarding the meaning of the sentence:

Among them is what al-Zamakhshari mentioned in al-Kashshaf, where he interpreted His saying: فَإِنْ يَشَإِ اللهُ يَخْتِمْ عَلى قَلْبِكَ by saying: "If Allah should wish, He would make you among those whose hearts are sealed so that you would fabricate a lie against Him, for no one fabricates a lie against Allah except one who is in such a state. The significance of this style is to deem fabrication from someone like him as highly improbable, and that it is as remote as associating partners with Allah and entering the group of those whose hearts are sealed. An example of this is when an honest person is accused of betrayal and says: 'Perhaps Allah has forsaken me, perhaps Allah has blinded my heart,' while he does not intend to affirm forsaking or blindness of the heart, but rather intends to deem it improbable that someone like him would betray, and to point out that his being accused of betrayal is a grave matter." [End of quote].

Among them is what has been said: The meaning is that if you were to suggest to yourself to fabricate a lie against Allah, Allah would stamp your heart and make you forget the Quran; so how could you be able to fabricate against Allah? This is like His saying: لَئِنْ أَشْرَكْتَ لَيَحْبَطَنَّ عَمَلُكَ — ‘If you ascribe partners [to Allah], your work will surely fail’ (39:65).

Among them is what has been said: Its meaning is that if Allah should wish, He would bind your heart with patience over their harm so that their saying "he is a fabricator and a sorcerer" would not be hard on you. These views are not free from weakness.

Among them is what has been said: Its meaning is that if Allah should wish, He would seal your heart just as He sealed their hearts, which is a consolation for the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) so that he may thank his Lord for the blessing He gave him.

Among them is what has been said: Its meaning is that if Allah should wish, He would seal the hearts of the disbelievers and their tongues and hasten their punishment. He shifted from the third person to the second person and from the plural to the singular, and the meaning is: He would seal your heart, O you who say: "He has fabricated a lie against Allah."

His saying: وَيَمْحُ اللهُ الْباطِلَ وَيُحِقُّ الْحَقَّ بِكَلِماتِهِ — Allah erases falsehood and confirms the truth by His words (42:24). The use of the present tense (المضارع)—"erases" (يمحو) and "confirms" (يحق)—is to indicate continuity (الاستمرار). Thus, the erasing of falsehood and the confirmation of truth by the words is a continuous sunnah (سنة) of His, the Exalted. What is meant by "the words" (الكلمات) is the divine revelation and Lordly speech sent down to the prophets. It is also possible that what is meant are the souls of the prophets, insofar as they express the hidden inner reality (الضمير الغيبي).

His saying: إِنَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ — Indeed He knows best what is in the breasts (42:24), is a justification for His saying: وَيَمْحُ اللهُ الْباطِلَ — Allah erases falsehood — etc. That is, He erases falsehood and confirms the truth by His words because He is knowledgeable of the hearts and what they contain; thus, He knows what they necessitate of guidance or misguidance, or expansion or sealing, through the sending down of revelation and the directing of the call.

It has been said: In the verse is an indication of a promise of victory to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and this is not without merit.

His saying, the Exalted: وَهُوَ الَّذِي يَقْبَلُ التَّوْبَةَ عَنْ عِبادِهِ وَيَعْفُوا عَنِ السَّيِّئاتِ وَيَعْلَمُ ما تَفْعَلُونَ — It is He who accepts the repentance of His servants, and excuses their misdeeds and He knows what you do (42:25). It is said: "accepted from him" (قبل منه) and "accepted on his behalf" (قبل عنه). The author of al-Kashshaf said: "It is said: 'I accepted the thing from (منه) him' and 'I accepted it on his behalf (عنه).' The meaning of 'I accepted it from him' is that I took it from him and made him the starting point and origin of my acceptance. The meaning of 'I accepted it on his behalf' is that I set it aside and separated it from him." [End of quote].

In His saying: وَيَعْلَمُ ما تَفْعَلُونَ — and He knows what you do — is an exhortation toward repentance and a warning against committing evil deeds. The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَسْتَجِيبُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ وَيَزِيدُهُمْ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ وَالْكافِرُونَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ — He answers those who have faith and do righteous deeds, and He enhances them out of His grace. As for the faithless, there is a severe punishment for them (42:26). The subject (فاعل) of answers (يَسْتَجِيبُ) is a pronoun referring back to Him, the Exalted, and those who have faith (الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا), etc., is in the position of the object (مفعول) by the removal of the preposition, and the estimation is "He answers to (للذين) those who have faith"—as has been said. It has also been said that the subject of answers is those (الَّذِينَ), but this is remote from the context.

Answering (الاستجابة) is the responding to a supplication (دعاء). Since worship (العبادة) is a call to Him, the Exalted, He expressed its acceptance as "answering" them. The evidence for this meaning is His saying: وَيَزِيدُهُمْ مِنْ فَضْلِهِ — and He enhances them out of His grace, for its apparent meaning is the increase of reward, as is the contrast of the answering of the believers with His saying: وَالْكافِرُونَ لَهُمْ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ — As for the faithless, there is a severe punishment for them.

It has been said: The meaning is that He answers them when they supplicate to Him and gives them what they asked for and increases for them beyond what they requested. This is remote from the context. Furthermore, the answering of supplication is not exclusive to the believer.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Majma', Zadan narrated from Ali (peace be upon him) that he said: "Regarding us, in Al-Ha-Mim (آل حم), there is a verse; no one preserves our affection except every believer." Then he recited: قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى — Say, ‘I do not ask you any reward for it except the love of [my] relatives’.

Al-Tabarsi said: "To this al-Kumayt alluded in his saying:

We found for you in Al-Ha-Mim a verse, Interpreted for us by the God-conscious and the eloquent."

In the same source, it is authentically reported from al-Hasan ibn Ali (peace be upon him) that he addressed the people and said in his sermon: "We are from the Household (أهل البيت) whose affection Allah has made obligatory upon every Muslim, for He said: قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Abdullah ibn Ajlan from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) regarding the saying of the Exalted: قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى, he said: "They are the Imams (الأئمة)."

I say: The reports with this meaning from the sources of the Shi'a from the Imams of the Household (أهل البيت) (peace be upon him) are very numerous, narrated from them.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ahmad, Abd ibn Humayd, al-Bukhari, Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, ibn Jarir, and ibn Mardawayh narrated through Tawus from ibn Abbas that he was asked about His saying: إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى. Sa'id ibn Jubayr said: "They are the relatives of the family of Muhammad." Ibn Abbas said: "You have been hasty. The Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) did not belong to a clan of Quraysh except that he had kinship among them." So he said: "Except that you maintain the kinship between me and you."

I say: It was also narrated from ibn Abbas through other chains besides this one. It has already been explained in the commentary on the verse that this meaning is not sound nor does it align with the context of the verse. What is strange is what appears in some of these chains: that the verse is abrogated (منسوخة) by His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ ما سَأَلْتُكُمْ مِنْ أَجْرٍ فَهُوَ لَكُمْ إِنْ أَجْرِيَ إِلَّا عَلَى اللهِ — Say, ‘Whatever reward I may have asked you is for your own selves; my reward lies only with Allah’ (34:47).

In the same source, Abu Nu'aym and al-Daylami narrated through Mujahid from ibn Abbas that he said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى — that you preserve me regarding my Household and love them for my sake."

In the same source, ibn al-Mundhir, ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani, and ibn Mardawayh narrated with a weak chain through Sa'id ibn Jubayr from ibn Abbas that he said: "When this verse was revealed: قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى, they said: 'O Messenger of Allah, who are these relatives of yours whose affection has become obligatory?' He said: 'Ali, Fatimah, and her two sons.'"

I say: Al-Tabarsi narrated it in al-Majma', and in it is "and her son" instead of "and her two sons."

In the same source, ibn Jarir narrated from Abu al-Daylam that he said: "When Ali ibn al-Husayn was brought as a captive and stood on the steps of Damascus, a man from the people of Syria stood up and said: 'Praise be to Allah who killed you and uprooted you.' Ali ibn al-Husayn said to him: 'Have you read the Quran?' He said: 'Yes.' He said: 'Have you read Al-Ha-Mim?' He said: 'Yes.' He said: 'Have you not read: قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى?' He said: 'Are you indeed them?' He said: 'Yes.'"

In the same source, ibn Abi Hatim narrated from ibn Abbas regarding وَمَنْ يَقْتَرِفْ حَسَنَةً — Whoever performs a good deed — he said: "Affection for the family of Muhammad."

I say: Something with this meaning was narrated in al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Muhammad ibn Muslim from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him).

In Tafsir al-Qummi, my father narrated to me from ibn Abi Najran from Asim ibn Humayd from Muhammad ibn Muslim that he said: "I heard Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) saying regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى, meaning regarding his Household (أهل بيته)."

He said: "The Ansar came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: 'We have given shelter and supported, so take a portion of our wealth and use it for what befalls you.' Then Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, sent down: قُلْ لا أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ أَجْراً إِلَّا الْمَوَدَّةَ فِي الْقُرْبى, meaning regarding his Household."

Then he said: "Do you not see that a man may have a friend, yet in that man's soul is something against his friend's household, so his breast is not at peace? Thus, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, willed that there be nothing in the soul of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) against his community, so Allah made the affection for the near of kin obligatory upon them. If they take it, they take an obligation, and if they leave it, they leave an obligation."

He said: "So they departed from him, some of them saying: 'We offered him our wealth, and he said: No. Fight for my Household after me.' And a group said: 'The Messenger of Allah did not say this,' and they denied it. They said as Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, narrated: أَمْ يَقُولُونَ افْتَرى عَلَى اللهِ كَذِباً — Do they say, ‘He has fabricated a lie against Allah’?. Then the Mighty and Majestic said: فَإِنْ يَشَإِ اللهُ يَخْتِمْ عَلى قَلْبِكَ — If Allah should wish, He will set a seal on your heart. He said: 'If you had fabricated.' وَيَمْحُ اللهُ الْباطِلَ — Allah erases falsehood — meaning He nullifies it. وَيُحِقُّ الْحَقَّ بِكَلِماتِهِ — and confirms the truth by His words — meaning by the Imams and the Riser (القائم) from the family of Muhammad (peace be upon him). إِنَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدُورِ — Indeed He knows best what is in the breasts."

I say: Al-Suyuti narrated the story of the Ansar in al-Durr al-Manthur from al-Tabarani and ibn Mardawayh through ibn Jubayr and weakened it.

Ayahs

۞ وَلَوْ بَسَطَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلرِّزْقَ لِعِبَادِهِۦ لَبَغَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَـٰكِن يُنَزِّلُ بِقَدَرٍ مَّا يَشَآءُ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ بِعِبَادِهِۦ خَبِيرٌۢ بَصِيرٌ (٢٧)
Were Allah to expand the provision for [all] His servants, they would surely create havoc on the earth. But He sends down in a [precise] measure whatever He wishes. Indeed, He is all-aware, all-seeing about His servants. (27)
وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُنَزِّلُ ٱلْغَيْثَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا قَنَطُوا۟ وَيَنشُرُ رَحْمَتَهُۥ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْوَلِىُّ ٱلْحَمِيدُ (٢٨)
It is He who sends down the rain after they have been despondent, and unfolds His mercy, and He is the Guardian, the All-laudable. (28)
وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ خَلْقُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَثَّ فِيهِمَا مِن دَآبَّةٍ ۚ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ جَمْعِهِمْ إِذَا يَشَآءُ قَدِيرٌ (٢٩)
Among His signs is the creation of the heavens and the earth and whatever creatures He has scattered in them, and He is able to gather them whenever He wishes. (29)
وَمَآ أَصَـٰبَكُم مِّن مُّصِيبَةٍ فَبِمَا كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَيَعْفُوا۟ عَن كَثِيرٍ (٣٠)
Whatever affliction that may visit you is because of what your hands have earned, and He excuses many [an offense of yours]. (30)
وَمَآ أَنتُم بِمُعْجِزِينَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ (٣١)
You cannot frustrate [Allah] on the earth, and you do not have besides Allah any guardian or helper. (31)
وَمِنْ ءَايَـٰتِهِ ٱلْجَوَارِ فِى ٱلْبَحْرِ كَٱلْأَعْلَـٰمِ (٣٢)
