The meaning would be: "Accordingly, We revealed the Quran to you by a Spirit from Us; you did not know what the Book was nor what faith was, but We made the Quran (or the Book) a light..." etc. This is an interpretation, though I do not recall any of the commentators mentioning it.

His saying: ما كُنْتَ تَدْرِي مَا الْكِتابُ وَلَا الْإِيمانُ — "You did not know what the Book is, nor what is faith." It has already been mentioned that the verse is intended to show that what he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله possesses and calls toward is from Allah the Glorified, not from himself, and that he was only given what he was given through revelation after his prophethood. Thus, the meaning of his "not knowing the Book" is his lack of knowledge regarding the details of the creedal knowledge (المعارف الاعتقادية) and practical laws (الشرائع العملية) contained within it, for that is what he was given knowledge of after prophethood and revelation. His "not knowing faith" means his not being characterized by the detailed commitment (التزام) to the true beliefs and righteous deeds. Action (العمل) has been called "faith" (إيمان) in His saying: وَما كانَ اللهُ لِيُضِيعَ إِيمانَكُمْ — "and Allah would not let your faith go to waste" (2:143).

The meaning, then, is: Before the revelation of the Spirit, you did not possess the Book with its knowledge and laws, nor were you characterized by what you are characterized by after the revelation—namely, the creedal and practical commitment to its contents. This does not contradict his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله being a believer in Allah and a monotheist (موحد) before his mission (البعثة), and righteous in his actions. What the verse negates is the knowledge of the details of what is in the Book and the commitment to them. The negation of detailed knowledge and commitment does not necessitate the negation of general (إجمالي) knowledge and commitment to faith in Allah and submission to the Truth.

By this, the argument of some who use the verse to claim that he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was not characterized by faith before his mission is refuted.

Also refuted is the claim of some that he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was always perfect in himself in knowledge and action, as this contradicts the apparent meaning of the verse: "You did not know what the Book is, nor what is faith."

The point of refutation is that it is necessary for there to be a difference in his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله state before prophethood and after it, and the verse points to this difference—that what he attained after prophethood was not of his own making, but was from Allah through the path of revelation.

His saying: وَلكِنْ جَعَلْناهُ نُوراً نَهْدِي بِهِ مَنْ نَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِنا — "but We made it a light by which We guide whomever We wish of Our servants." The pronoun in "We made it" (جَعَلْناهُ) refers to the Spirit. The meaning of "whomever We wish" (مَنْ نَشاءُ)—on the assumption that the Spirit refers to the Quran—is the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and those who believe in him, for they are all guided by the Quran.

On the assumption that it refers to the Spirit of the Command, then "whomever We wish" refers to all the Prophets and those who believe in them from their nations, for He guides through the revelation brought by it both the Prophets and the believers of their nations, and He specifically directs the Prophets and guides them toward righteous deeds and inspires them with them.

Accordingly, the verse is in the position of confirming the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله—confirming him in his claim that his Book is from Allah by His revelation, and confirming him in his claim that he is a believer in what he calls toward. It thus carries the meaning of the Exalted's saying: إِنَّكَ لَمِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ عَلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ تَنْزِيلَ الْعَزِيزِ الرَّحِيمِ — "You are indeed one of the apostles, on a straight path. [It is] a revelation sent down by the All-mighty, the All-merciful" (36:3-5).

His saying: وَإِنَّكَ لَتَهْدِي إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ — "and indeed you guide to a straight path" (42:52) is an indication that what he guides toward is a straight path, and that those among the people whom he guides are those whom Allah the Glorified guides. Thus, his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله guidance is the guidance of Allah.

His saying, the Exalted: صِراطِ اللهِ الَّذِي لَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ — "the path of Allah, to whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth" (42:53) is an explanation of the "straight path" to which the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله guides. Describing Him, the Exalted, with the phrase "to whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth" serves to provide the proof for the straightness of His path. Since He, the Exalted, owns everything, He owns the goal (الغاية) toward which things travel and the felicity (السعادة) toward which they turn. Thus, the goal and felicity are what He has determined, and the path to them and the way they must traverse to attain their felicity is what He has legislated and clarified. No one owns anything such that they could establish for it a goal and an end, or legislate for it a way toward it. Therefore, the felicity to which the Glorified calls is the true felicity, and the path to which He calls is the true path and the straight path.

His saying: أَلا إِلَى اللهِ تَصِيرُ الْأُمُورُ — "Behold! To Allah do all matters return" (42:53) is a reminder of the necessary consequence of His ownership of what is in the heavens and the earth, for its consequence is the return of their matters to Him. A further consequence is that the path they traverse—which is among their matters—returns to Him. Thus, the straight path is His path. The present tense verb "return" (تَصِيرُ) denotes continuity (الاستمرار).

In this, there is an allusion to the "why" (لم) of revelation and divine speaking. Since the return of all things is to Allah the Exalted, every species has a path toward Allah the Exalted that it traverses, and it is incumbent upon Him to guide it to it and drive it toward its goal, as He said: وَعَلَى اللهِ قَصْدُ السَّبِيلِ — "With Allah rests the [guidance to the] straight path" (16:9). This is the "speaking" to every species in a manner suited to its essence, which in the human being is the speaking called "revelation" and "sending messengers."

It has been said that the present tense verb denotes the future, referring to the return of all things to Him on the Day of Resurrection. The sentence would then be intended as a promise to those guided to the straight path and a threat to those who stray from it. The first of the two views is more apparent.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Durr al-Manthur, it is narrated by al-Bukhari, Muslim, and al-Bayhaqi from Aisha that al-Harith ibn Hisham asked the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "How does the revelation come to you?" He said: أحيانا يأتيني الملك في مثل صلصلة الجرس ـ فيفصم عني وقد وعيت عنه ما قال وهو أشده علي ، وأحيانا يتمثل لي الملك رجلا فيكلمني فأعي ما يقول — "Sometimes the angel comes to me like the ringing of a bell—then it leaves me while I have grasped what he said, and that is the hardest upon me. And sometimes the angel takes the form of a man for me and speaks to me, and I grasp what he says."

Aisha said: "I have indeed seen him when the revelation was descending upon him on a severely cold day—then it would leave him—and his forehead would be dripping with sweat."

In al-Tawhid, with its chain of narration from Zurarah, who said: "I said to Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): 'May I be your ransom—the fainting (الغشية) that used to afflict the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله when the revelation descended upon him?' He said: ذلك إذا لم يكن بينه وبين الله أحد ـ ذاك إذا تجلى الله له — 'That was when there was no one between him and Allah—that was when Allah manifested Himself to him.' He then said: تلك النبوة يا زرارة — 'That is prophethood, O Zurarah,' and he began to show great humility."

In al-Ilal, with its chain of narration from Ibn Abi Umayr from Amr ibn Jami' from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: كان جبرئيل إذا أتى النبي صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله قعد بين يديه قعدة العبد ، وكان لا يدخل حتى يستأذنه — "Whenever Gabriel came to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, he would sit before him as a servant sits, and he would not enter until he had asked his permission."

In the Amali of the Shaykh, with its chain of narration from Ibn Abi Umayr from Hisham ibn Salim from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "One of our companions said: 'May Allah set your affairs right—the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله used to say: "Gabriel said," and "This is Gabriel commanding me"—yet at other times he would be in a state where he would lose consciousness?' Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) said: أنه إذا كان الوحي من الله إليه ـ ليس بينهما جبرئيل ـ أصابه ذلك لثقل الوحي من الله ، وإذا كان بينهما جبرئيل لم يصبه ذلك ـ فقال : قال لي جبرئيل وهذا جبرئيل — 'When the revelation was from Allah to him—without Gabriel between them—that would afflict him due to the weight of the revelation from Allah. But when Gabriel was between them, that would not afflict him—so he would say: "Gabriel said to me" and "This is Gabriel."'"

In al-Basa'ir, from Ali ibn Hassan from Ibn Bukayr from Zurarah, who said: "I asked Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): 'Who is the Messenger (الرسول)? Who is the Prophet (النبي)? Who is the one spoken to (المحدث)?' He said: الرسول الذي يأتيه جبرئيل فيكلمه قبلا ـ فيراه كما يرى أحدكم صاحبه الذي يكلمه فهذا الرسول ، والنبي الذي يؤتى في النوم نحو رؤيا إبراهيم عليه‌السلام ، ونحو ما كان يأخذ رسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله من السبات ـ إذا أتاه جبرئيل في النوم فهكذا النبي ، ومنهم من يجمع له الرسالة والنبوة ـ فكان رسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله رسولا نبيا ـ يأتيه جبرئيل قبلا فيكمله ويراه ، ويأتيه في النوم ، وأما المحدث فهو الذي يسمع كلام الملك ـ فيحدثه من غير أن يراه ـ ومن غير أن يأتيه في النوم — 'The Messenger is he to whom Gabriel comes and speaks face-to-face—he sees him just as one of you sees his companion with whom he speaks; this is the Messenger. The Prophet is he who is visited in sleep, like the vision of Abraham (peace be upon him), and like the slumber that used to take the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله—when Gabriel came to him in sleep, such is the Prophet. Among them are those for whom both the Message and Prophethood are combined—so the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was a Messenger-Prophet—Gabriel would come to him face-to-face, speak to him and he would see him, and he would come to him in sleep. As for the one spoken to (المحدث), he is the one who hears the speech of the angel—who speaks to him without him seeing the angel—and without him coming to him in sleep.'"

I say: There are other narrations with the same meaning.

In al-Tawhid, with its chain of narration from Muhammad ibn Muslim and Muhammad ibn Marwan from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: ما علم رسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله أن جبرئيل من قبل الله إلا بالتوفيق — "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله did not know that Gabriel was from Allah except through [divine] success (التوفيق)."

In the Tafsir of al-Ayyashi, from Zurarah, who said: "I said to Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): 'How did the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله not fear—regarding what came to him from Allah—that it might be from the incitements of Satan?' He said: إن الله إذا اتخذ عبدا رسولا ـ أنزل عليه السكينة والوقار ـ فكان يأتيه من قبل الله مثل الذي يراه بعينه — 'Indeed, when Allah takes a servant as a Messenger, He sends down upon him tranquility (السكينة) and dignity (الوقار), so that what comes to him from Allah is like that which he sees with his own eyes.'"

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Abu Basir, who said: "I asked Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) about the saying of Allah, Blessed and Exalted: وَكَذلِكَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ رُوحاً مِنْ أَمْرِنا ما كُنْتَ تَدْرِي مَا الْكِتابُ وَلَا الْإِيمانُ — 'Accordingly We have revealed to you a spirit of Our command. You did not know what the Book is, nor what is faith.' He said: خلق من خلق أعظم من جبرئيل وميكائيل ـ كان مع رسول الله صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله يخبره ويسدده ، وهو مع الأئمة من بعده — 'A creation among the creations, greater than Gabriel and Michael—it was with the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله informing him and directing him, and it is with the Imams after him.'"

I say: There are several narrations with this meaning, and in some of them, it is stated that it is from the celestial realm (الملكوت). The author of Ruh al-Ma'ani said: "Al-Tabarsi narrated from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah: 'The intended meaning of this Spirit is an angel greater than Gabriel and Michael—it was with the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and did not ascend to heaven.' This statement is extremely strange and perhaps it is not authentic from these two Imams." End quote.

What is in Majma' al-Bayan is: "From Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdillah (peace be upon them), they said: 'And it did not ascend to heaven, and it is indeed within us.'" End quote.

His finding it strange, while he has no evidence to negate it, is itself strange. Furthermore, he accepts the direction (تسديد) of this Spirit for some of the Ummah other than the Prophet, as is apparent to whoever consults the section of "Allusions" (الإشارات) in his commentary.

In Nahj al-Balagha: ولقد قرن الله به صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله من لدن كان فطيما ـ أعظم ملك من ملائكته يسلك به طريق المكارم ـ ومحاسن أخلاق العالم ليله ونهاره — "And Allah had coupled with him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, from the time he was weaned, the greatest angel among His angels, to lead him along the path of noble traits and the best ethics of the world, night and day."

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Abu Nu'aym in al-Dala'il and Ibn Asakir narrated from Ali, who said: "It was said to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: 'Did you ever worship an idol?' He said: لا — 'No.' They said: 'Did you ever drink wine?' He said: لا. وما زلت أعرف أن الذي هم عليه كفر ـ وما كنت أدري ما الكتاب وما الإيمان ، وبذلك نزل القرآن : ما كُنْتَ تَدْرِي مَا الْكِتابُ وَلَا الْإِيمانُ — 'No. I always knew that what they were upon was disbelief—though I did not know what the Book was nor what faith was, and thus the Quran was revealed: "You did not know what the Book is, nor what is faith."'"

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Abu Amr al-Zubayri from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him) in a tradition, he said regarding His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: وَإِنَّكَ لَتَهْدِي إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ — "and indeed you guide to a straight path." He said: يقول : تدعو — "He means: You call."

In al-Kafi, with its chain of narration from Jabir from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him), who said: "I heard him saying: وقع مصحف في البحر فوجدوه ـ وقد ذهب ما فيه إلا هذه الآية : أَلا إِلَى اللهِ تَصِيرُ الْأُمُورُ — 'A copy of the Quran fell into the sea and they found it—and all that was in it had vanished except for this verse: "Behold! To Allah do all matters return."'"
